### Orleans streaming quickstart > Produce events

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-quick-start

Producing events for Orleans streams involves obtaining access to a configured stream provider, selecting a specific stream using a GUID and an optional namespace, and then using the `OnNextAsync` method to send data. Streams are identified by a GUID and a namespace, allowing for unique identification, such as using a chat room's namespace and the owning grain's GUID.

### Orleans silo lifecycle overview > Logging

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/silo-lifecycle

To aid in understanding the startup and shutdown order due to Orleans's inversion of control, Orleans logs component participation at each lifecycle stage. These logs, recorded at the Information level for Orleans.Runtime.SiloLifecycleSubject, detail which components are active at specific stages and the time taken for them to start.

### Introduction to Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans is a programming model for building distributed applications. It provides a straightforward approach to building applications that span multiple machines and services.

### Grain lifecycle overview > Stages

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

The predefined grain lifecycle stages include `First` (the initial stage), `SetupState` (for setting up grain state, including loading from storage for stateful grains), `Activate` (where Orleans calls `OnActivateAsync` and `OnDeactivateAsync`), and `Last` (the final stage).

--------------------------------

### Request context

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/request-context

While any serializable object can be used as application metadata, using large or complex objects may increase message serialization time. It is recommended to use simple data types like strings, GUIDs, or numeric types to minimize overhead.

### Orleans streaming APIs > Async stream

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-programming-apis

The Orleans.Streams.IAsyncStream<T> interface provides a logical, strongly typed handle to a virtual stream. Calls to obtain stream providers and stream handles are local operations. Stream identities, formed by a GUID and a namespace, offer flexibility, allowing multiple streams with the same GUID to exist across different namespaces.

### Orleans lifecycle overview > Utilities > Extensions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

Helper extension functions simplify common lifecycle usage patterns. These extensions allow components to subscribe to an observable lifecycle using lambdas or member functions instead of implementing the ILifecycleObserver interface directly. Similar extensions also support using generic type arguments.

--------------------------------

### Custom serialization introduction

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Orleans serialization involves three stages: deep copying objects for isolation, serializing objects into a byte stream before transmission, and deserializing objects from the byte stream upon delivery. Data types used in messages must have associated routines for these three steps, collectively known as serializers. The copier is independent, while the serializer and deserializer work as a pair.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization Customization > Write a serializer for an individual type

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

To write a serializer for an individual type, create a new class annotated with `[SerializerAttribute(typeof(TargetType))]`, where `TargetType` is the type to be serialized. This class must implement three serialization routines: a copier, a serializer, and a deserializer. The rules for these routines are the same as when implementing `IExternalSerializer`. The `[SerializerAttribute]` can be specified multiple times on the same class if it can serialize multiple types.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Serialization best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

When serializing types in Orleans, it's recommended to use aliases with the `[Alias("my-type")]` attribute to allow renaming types without breaking compatibility. Avoid changing a `record` to a `class` or vice-versa, as they are not represented identically. Do not add new types to an existing type hierarchy, but adding new subclasses is safe. Replace `SerializableAttribute` with `GenerateSerializerAttribute` and `IdAttribute` declarations. Start all member IDs at zero for each type, and note that IDs can safely overlap between base and subclasses.

--------------------------------

### Orleans lifecycle overview > Utilities > Lifecycle participation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

A lifecycle participant marker interface, ILifecycleParticipant, helps identify components interested in participating in a lifecycle. This interface is used to recognize which components should be involved in the lifecycle management of other components, particularly relevant for silo and grain lifecycles.

--------------------------------

### Stream identities

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

In Orleans 7.0, stream identities are now represented using strings instead of Guids. The `StreamId` struct includes `Namespace`, `Key`, and `FullKey` properties, all of which are UTF-8 encoded strings.

--------------------------------

### Orleans lifecycle overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

The Orleans lifecycle pattern addresses the need for ordered startup and shutdown of complex components such as grains, silos, and clients. It consists of an observable lifecycle, which signals startup and shutdown stages, and lifecycle observers, which perform operations at specific stages.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Serialization best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

When defining serializable types in Orleans, it is recommended to use the `[Alias("my-type")]` attribute to allow renaming types without breaking compatibility. Avoid changing a `record` to a `class` or vice-versa, as they are not represented identically. Do not add new types to an existing type hierarchy, but adding new subclasses is safe. Replace `SerializableAttribute` with `GenerateSerializerAttribute` and `IdAttribute` declarations. Start all member IDs at zero for each type, as IDs can safely overlap between subclasses and their base classes.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans documentation > Featured content > Work with grains

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans

Orleans supports various features for managing grain state and behavior, such as grain persistence, grain event sourcing, grain versioning, grain services, grain observers, and grain interceptors. These features enable robust and flexible application development.

--------------------------------

### Implementation details overview > Scheduler

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation

The Orleans Scheduler is a core component within the Orleans runtime. Its primary responsibility is to execute application code and parts of the runtime code, ensuring single-threaded execution semantics.

--------------------------------

### Orleans silo lifecycle overview > Stages

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/silo-lifecycle

Orleans silos and cluster clients utilize a defined set of service lifecycle stages for managing startup and shutdown. These stages include First, RuntimeInitialize (for threading), RuntimeServices (for networking and agents), RuntimeStorageServices, RuntimeGrainServices (for grain management, membership, and directory), ApplicationServices, BecomeActive (joining the cluster), Active (ready for workload), and Last.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/orleans-architecture-principles-and-approach

Explore external links for .NET Orleans.

--------------------------------

### Orleans lifecycle overview > Lifecycle observer

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

Components that need to participate in another component's lifecycle must implement the ILifecycleObserver interface, providing hooks for their startup and shutdown behaviors. They then subscribe to a specific stage of an observable lifecycle. Both OnStart and OnStop methods are called when the subscribed stage is reached during the respective phases of the lifecycle.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/deploying-new-versions-of-grains

Explore various aspects of .NET Orleans, including version selector strategy, compatible grains, backward compatibility guidelines, grain interface versioning, silo error code monitoring, failure handling, and silo lifecycles.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Copiers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans serializes objects by copying them by default to prevent concurrency bugs. The `Orleans.Serialization` facilitates this copying. Applying the `Orleans.CodeGeneration.GenerateSerializerAttribute` to a type also generates copiers for it. Types or members marked with `ImmutableAttribute` are not copied.

--------------------------------

### Best practices in Orleans > Grains overview > Asynchronicity

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

In Orleans, all asynchronous operations must use the Task Asynchronous Programming (TAP) pattern, and 'await' is the preferred syntax for composing async operations. Common scenarios include returning a concrete value using Task.FromResult, returning a Task of the same type, awaiting a Task and continuing execution, or using fan-out patterns with Task.WhenAll for parallel execution.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/overview-helloworld

To run the sample app, refer to the Readme file.

### Orleans lifecycle overview > Observable lifecycle

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

An observable lifecycle allows other components to observe its stages and receive notifications during startup and shutdown. Components can subscribe to a specific stage, and observers are notified when that stage is reached. Stages are processed in ascending order during startup and descending order during shutdown. Observers can unsubscribe by disposing of the returned disposable object.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle overview > Memory-based activation shedding > Best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

When configuring memory-based activation shedding, it's recommended to set thresholds conservatively to avoid oscillation, monitor collection metrics to understand workload impact, consider the importance of critical grains, and test under load to verify behavior under production-like memory conditions.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Copiers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans prioritizes safety by default, including protection against concurrency bugs. It achieves this by immediately copying objects passed in grain calls. The Orleans.Serialization library facilitates this copying. Applying the `Orleans.CodeGeneration.GenerateSerializerAttribute` to a type also generates copiers for it. Types or members marked with `ImmutableAttribute` are exempt from copying.

It also emphasizes extensibility without breaking the framework, allowing for custom providers, and adheres to the principle of least surprise by maintaining familiarity and predictable behavior.

### Overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

While a grain call is executing, the runtime listens for cancellation and propagates the signal to the remote caller. Cancellation signals are only propagated while the call is active. Once a call completes, subsequent cancellation signals are not propagated.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

Grains are the fundamental primitives in Orleans applications. They are classes that inherit from the `Grain` base class and implement interfaces to define their grain key identifier. Common interfaces for grain keys include `IGrainWithGuidKey`, `IGrainWithIntegerKey`, and `IGrainWithStringKey`. Grains can also define custom interfaces to specify their methods and properties, such as methods for persisting and retrieving data.

### Grain directory implementation > Overview and architecture

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/grain-directory

Orleans transitioned from an eventually consistent, distributed hash table directory to a strongly consistent one in v9.0, based on the two-phase Virtually Synchronous methodology. This newer implementation also uses a distributed hash table but enhances load balancing through virtual nodes.

This, combined with asynchronous messaging and efficient scheduling, enables significant distributed parallelism and overall throughput without requiring explicit multi-threading.

### Orleans architecture design principles

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/orleans-architecture-principles-and-approach

Orleans is guided by architectural principles focused on the 80% use case, prioritizing scalability and availability above raw performance. It adopts a recovery-oriented computing approach, emphasizing detection and fixing of problems rather than attempting 100% prevention, acknowledging that failures are inevitable at cloud scale. This approach contrasts with traditional fault tolerance, which can be fragile.

### Subscribe to and receive streaming data

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-quick-start

For receiving data in Orleans, you can use implicit and explicit subscriptions. Implicit subscriptions are simpler and are enabled by decorating a grain type with the `[ImplicitStreamSubscription(namespace)]` attribute. When data is pushed to streams in a specified namespace, a grain with the same Guid as the stream receives the message. The runtime automatically creates a new activation for the grain if none exists.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Orleans grains utilize an observable lifecycle for ordered activation and deactivation. This mechanism ensures that grain logic, system components, and application logic start and stop in a controlled sequence during grain activation and collection.

--------------------------------

### Orleans silo lifecycle overview > Silo lifecycle participation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/silo-lifecycle

Application logic can integrate into the Orleans silo's lifecycle by registering an ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle> service in the silo's service container. When the silo starts, the Participate method of each registered participant is invoked, allowing them to hook into the observable lifecycle stages as they execute in order.

--------------------------------

### Overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

Cancellation in Orleans is cooperative, meaning grain implementations must actively check and respond to the CancellationToken for it to be effective. If a token is not observed, the runtime will not automatically stop a method. This .NET standard allows for cleanup logic when cancellation is signaled.

--------------------------------

### Overview of GrainCancellationToken

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

GrainCancellationToken is a wrapper around the standard CancellationToken, enabling cooperative cancellation between threads, thread pool work items, or Task objects. A GrainCancellationTokenSource provides a GrainCancellationToken through its Token property and initiates cancellation when its Cancel method is called.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

When serializing generic types, the `TargetType` parameter of `[Serializer(typeof(TargetType))]` can be an open-generic type like `MyGenericType<T>`. In this scenario, the serializer class must mirror the generic parameters of the target type. Orleans will generate a concrete serializer at runtime for each specific `MyGenericType<T>` that is used, such as `MyGenericType<int>` or `MyGenericType<string>`.

Connection strings contain secrets and should be avoided in production. Use managed identity whenever possible.

### Best practices

Share common configurations, including OpenTelemetry and health checks, across all projects by implementing a dedicated ServiceDefaults project. This promotes consistency and simplifies management.

### Configuration Guide > Activation Collection > Keep alive

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/activation-collection

The `Orleans.KeepAliveAttribute` can be applied to a grain implementation to prevent it from being collected by the idle activation collector. This is useful for grains that are used infrequently but need to remain active to avoid potential creation overhead on subsequent invocations.

--------------------------------

### Grain identity > Grain keys > Using globally unique identifiers (GUIDs) as keys

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-identity

Using `System.Guid` as keys is beneficial when randomness and global uniqueness are required, such as in job processing systems. This avoids the need for coordinating key allocation, which can lead to bottlenecks.

--------------------------------

### Orleans Client Libraries

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Client` meta-package simplifies building and starting an Orleans client (frontend). It includes `Microsoft.Orleans.Core.Abstractions`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Core`, and `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansProviders`.

--------------------------------

### Grain references

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-references

A grain reference contains three key pieces of information: the grain type (which uniquely identifies the grain class), the grain key (which uniquely identifies a logical instance of that grain class), and the interface the grain reference must implement. The grain type and key together form the grain identity.

--------------------------------

### External tasks and grains

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/external-tasks-and-grains

By design, any sub-tasks spawned from grain code, such as those using `await`, `ContinueWith`, or `Task.Factory.StartNew`, dispatch on the same per-activation TaskScheduler as the parent task. This ensures they inherit the single-threaded execution model of the grain code, which is fundamental to Orleans' turn-based concurrency.

--------------------------------

### Streaming with Orleans > Programming model

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming

The Orleans Streams Programming Model is based on several key principles: streams are virtual and always exist, identified by logical names (GUIDs and strings); they decouple data generation from processing, allowing for distribution and fault tolerance; they are lightweight and dynamic, handling a high rate of stream creation and destruction; bindings are dynamic, allowing grains to connect and disconnect frequently; the runtime transparently manages consumption lifecycles, ensuring event delivery even through failures; and streams work uniformly across grains and clients.

--------------------------------

### Request context

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/request-context

Application metadata is preserved when scheduling future computations using StartNew or ContinueWith. The continuation will execute with the same metadata that was present when the computation was scheduled. The system copies the current metadata and passes it to the continuation, ensuring it does not reflect changes made after the call to StartNew or ContinueWith.

--------------------------------

### Orleans migration guides > Migrate from Orleans 7.0 to 10.0 > Breaking changes summary

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

Several breaking changes require attention during migration. These include the removal of `AddGrainCallFilter` (use `AddIncomingGrainCallFilter`), a typo fix in `LeaseAquisitionPeriod` to `LeaseAcquisitionPeriod`, and a rename of `LoadSheddingLimit` to `CpuThreshold`. The default for `CancelRequestOnTimeout` has changed, and the ADO.NET provider now requires `Microsoft.Data.SqlClient`. Obsolete attributes like `[Unordered]` and `OrleansConstructorAttribute` should be removed or replaced, and `RegisterTimer` should be updated to `RegisterGrainTimer`.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle overview > Memory-based activation shedding > Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Memory-based activation shedding is configured using `GrainCollectionOptions`, where you can enable shedding, set the memory usage percentage thresholds for triggering and targeting collection, and define the polling period for memory usage checks.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Microsoft Orleans is a cross-platform framework designed to simplify building distributed applications. It scales elastically from a single server to thousands of cloud-based apps, providing tools to manage distributed system complexities. Orleans extends C# concepts to multi-server environments, supports cloud-native deployment on various platforms like Linux, Windows, and macOS, and integrates with modern options such as Kubernetes and Azure services.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

For users migrating from Orleans 3.x, follow the specific migration guidance provided in the Orleans 7.0 documentation. Orleans 3.x is no longer supported, and users are encouraged to migrate to Orleans 10.0 for the latest features and security updates.

The Orleans team provides resources covering the architecture, principles, and approach of Orleans, along with discussions on building real applications and cloud services at scale.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

The integration of Orleans with .NET Aspire is facilitated by the `Aspire.Hosting.Orleans` package within your AppHost project. This package provides extension methods for defining Orleans as a distributed resource, configuring various providers for clustering, grain storage, reminders, and grain directories, and modeling silo and client relationships.

### Hints for writing serializers and deserializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

For classes with complex internal structures, such as dictionaries in Orleans, an effective approach is to use instance methods to access and serialize the object's semantic content, and then deserialize by setting this semantic content rather than the intricate internal state. This method utilizes `SerializeInner` and `DeserializeInner` for inner objects and often requires a custom copier.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle overview > Memory-based activation shedding

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Memory-based activation shedding automatically deactivates grain activations when a silo experiences memory pressure. This feature helps prevent out-of-memory conditions by intelligently removing grains from memory based on their activity patterns, prioritizing older and less-recently-used grains.

--------------------------------

### Design rationale > Portability and flexibility

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The `IMembershipTable` abstraction in Orleans promotes portability and flexibility, adhering to the philosophy of not forcing strong dependencies on specific technologies. This design allows different components to be easily switched with alternative implementations, providing a flexible architecture.

--------------------------------

### Heterogeneous silos overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/heterogeneous-silos

In a heterogeneous silo setup, different silos within a single cluster can host distinct sets of grain types. For example, Silo 1 and 2 might support grain types A and B, Silo 3 supports grain type C, Silo 4 supports grain type D, and Silo 5 supports grain type E. All silos must reference the interfaces of all grain types in the cluster, but grain classes should only be referenced by the silos that host them. Clients are unaware of which specific silo hosts a particular grain type, and importantly, a grain type's implementation must be identical across all silos that support it.

--------------------------------

### Relational Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

The interface between Orleans and the database should adhere to a minimum set of abstractions and rules to ensure robustness, facilitate testing, and accommodate potential changes in deployment environments or vendor-specific features. Orleans operates on column names and types for reading data, and parameter names and types for writing.

--------------------------------

### Properties of the membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The membership table serves as a valuable diagnostic tool, providing system administrators with an instantaneous list of alive silos and a history of killed silos and suspicions, which is crucial for troubleshooting.

--------------------------------

### Heterogeneous silos overview > Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/heterogeneous-silos

Deploying heterogeneous silos requires no special configuration; you can deploy different binaries on each silo. However, the interval for checking changes in supported types can be adjusted using the TypeManagementOptions.TypeMapRefreshInterval property. For testing, the GrainClassOptions.ExcludedGrainTypes property allows you to specify grain types to exclude from specific silos.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans documentation > Featured content > Deploy Orleans apps

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans

Deploying Orleans applications can be done on various platforms, including Kubernetes, Service Fabric, Consul, Azure App Service, and Azure Container Apps. Each deployment option offers different benefits for managing and scaling your distributed applications.

--------------------------------

### Configure Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/consul-deployment

For clients interested in using Consul for service discovery, the same NuGet package should be referenced, and the UseConsulClientClustering extension method should be called.

--------------------------------

### Hosting > Orleans Runtime Abstractions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Runtime.Abstractions` package provides interfaces and abstractions for types implemented in `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansRuntime`.

--------------------------------

### Design goals > 2. Maintain potential to tune queries and database structure, even while a deployment is running

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

The system is designed to allow tuning of queries and database structure even while a deployment is running. This is crucial in managed hosting environments where altering Orleans or application binaries might be restricted, but database parameters can often be adjusted.

--------------------------------

### Grain identity

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-identity

Grains can be identified using various primary key types, including long integers, strings, and compound keys. Long integers are suitable for relational database persistence, while strings offer flexibility. Compound keys allow a combination of a GUID or long with a string for more complex identification scenarios.

--------------------------------

### Blazor Server and Blazor WebAssembly

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

Blazor samples, adapted from introductory tutorials, integrate with Orleans. They include interactive counters, TODO lists, and weather services, with separate samples for Blazor Server and Blazor WebAssembly hosting models.

--------------------------------

### Frequently Asked Questions > Positioning > What is the difference between Orleans and other actor languages and frameworks, such as Erlang or Akka?

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/frequently-asked-questions

While sharing principles with other actor frameworks like Erlang or Akka, Orleans distinguishes itself by introducing Virtual Actors. This concept simplifies developer experience and makes it highly suitable for cloud services and large-scale systems.

--------------------------------

### Monitoring Orleans > Catalog

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

The Orleans catalog layer provides meters to observe the number of catalog activations, activations within the working set, created and destroyed activations, failed activations, idle activation collections, shutdown activations, non-existent activations, and concurrent activation registration attempts.

--------------------------------

### Put it all together

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

After registering the grain storage, grains can be decorated with the `[StorageProvider(ProviderName = "File")]` attribute to store their state in the specified root directory.

--------------------------------

### Migration Guide > Rolling upgrades

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

Rolling upgrades from Orleans 7.x to 10.0 are not recommended due to significant protocol and API changes. The recommended strategy involves deploying a new cluster running Orleans 10.0, migrating state data if necessary, switching traffic to the new cluster, and then decommissioning the old cluster.

The Bicep file defines a storage account resource with the name, location, kind, and SKU specified. It also outputs a connection string for the storage account, constructed using the account name, key, and environment suffix.

The `ConfigureApplicationParts` option is used to add grain class and interface assemblies as application parts. This is not required in Orleans 7.0+ as source generators handle this automatically.

### Adventure game sample project

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/adventure

The AdventureServer program reads an AdventureMap.json file to set up game 'rooms' like forests and beaches, which connect to form the game's layout. Rooms can also contain 'things' such as keys or swords. The AdventureClient program establishes the player and provides a text-based interface for interaction.

### Design goals > 3. Allow use of vendor-specific and version-specific abilities

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

The design enables the utilization of vendor-specific and version-specific database features, such as native UPSERT commands or specialized partitioning schemes. This allows applications to take advantage of unique capabilities offered by different database systems for improved performance and functionality.

--------------------------------

### Write state

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

Writing the state involves serializing the state using `_jsonSettings` and constructing the file path. An ETag check is performed against the file's last updated UTC time to detect concurrent modifications. If a conflict is detected, an `InconsistentStateException` is thrown, leading to the current activation being killed to prevent data loss.

--------------------------------

### Orleans migration guides > Migrate from Orleans 7.0 to 10.0 > Package updates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

To migrate from Orleans 7.x to 10.0, update your NuGet package references. Key packages like `Microsoft.Orleans.Server`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Client`, and `Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk` should be updated to their 10.0.0 versions. Streaming and persistence packages such as `Microsoft.Orleans.Streaming.EventHubs`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Streaming.AzureStorage`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Persistence.AzureStorage`, and `Microsoft.Orleans.Clustering.AzureStorage` also need to be updated.

--------------------------------

Develop a declarative dataflow language over Orleans streams, similar to Trident-Storm, and an optimizer to configure stream processing for minimal cost.

### Application parts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

The `ConfigureApplicationParts` method accepts a delegate, `Action<IApplicationPartManager>`, which provides access to extension methods for managing application parts. Common methods include `AddApplicationPart` to add a single assembly, `AddFromAppDomain` to add all assemblies in the current `AppDomain`, and `AddFromApplicationBaseDirectory` to load assemblies from the current base path.

--------------------------------

### Orleans configuration guide

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide

Silos and clients in Orleans can be configured programmatically using `UseOrleans(IHostBuilder, Action<HostBuilderContext,ISiloBuilder>)` and `UseOrleansClient`, respectively. This configuration is achieved through supplemental option classes that follow the .NET Options pattern. These option classes can be loaded from various sources, including files, environment variables, or any other valid configuration provider.

--------------------------------

### The actor model

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans is based on the actor model, a concept originating in the early 1970s. In this model, each actor is a lightweight, concurrent, immutable object that encapsulates state and behavior. Actors communicate exclusively through asynchronous messages. Orleans introduced the Virtual Actor abstraction, where actors exist perpetually and are always addressable, unaffected by server failures.

--------------------------------

### Azure Queue streams overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/streams-implementation/azure-queue-streams

Pulling agents repeatedly fetch messages from queues until none are left, then pause for a configurable duration. They store messages in a cache for consumers, pausing reads if the cache becomes full. Message removal from the cache is triggered by consumer processing, effectively throttling the read rate by the consumer's processing speed.

--------------------------------

### Prepare for Azure deployment

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

To successfully run the deployment workflow, ensure that the service principal has the necessary provider namespaces registered: Microsoft.Web, Microsoft.Network, Microsoft.OperationalInsights, Microsoft.Insights, and Microsoft.Storage.

--------------------------------

### Troubleshoot Azure Cloud Service deployments > Improperly modified configuration files

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/troubleshooting-azure-cloud-services-deployments

Ensure proper endpoints are configured in the _ServiceDefinition.csdef_ file to avoid deployment failures and errors related to obtaining endpoint information.

--------------------------------

### Membership table

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The IMembershipTable interface serves as a central point for silos to discover each other and for clients to locate silos. It also plays a role in coordinating the membership view agreement among silos.

--------------------------------

### What are grains?

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

A grain is the fundamental building block in any Orleans application and represents a virtual actor. Grains are entities with user-defined identity, behavior, and state. They can be invoked by other grains or external clients and implement interfaces like IGrainWithGuidKey or IGrainWithIntegerKey. Grains can have volatile or persistent state, and the Orleans runtime automatically manages their lifecycle, activating them on demand and removing them from memory when inactive.

--------------------------------

### Data mapping

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence

Individual storage providers determine the best way to store grain state, with common choices being blob serialization or column-per-field storage.

--------------------------------

### AppHost extension methods reference

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

When configuring a backing resource with methods like `.WithClustering(resource)` or `.WithGrainStorage(name, resource)`, the Orleans resource automatically includes a reference to that backing resource. You only need to call `.WithReference(orleans)` once. Use `.WaitFor()` on the backing resource to ensure it's ready before the silo starts.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Versioning rules > Numerics

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

When modifying numeric fields, their signedness cannot be changed (e.g., `int` to `uint` is invalid). However, the width of numeric fields can be changed. Conversions that narrow the width are supported but will throw an exception if the runtime value causes an overflow. Specific rules apply to conversions between floating-point types and decimal types based on their range.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

The `az deployment group create` command evaluates a Bicep file, which contains declarative information about the Azure resources to deploy. This process is referred to as provisioning all resources for deployment. The Bicep authoring experience is enhanced with the Bicep Extension in Visual Studio Code.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Serialization best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

For numeric members, it is safe to widen their types (e.g., `sbyte` to `short` to `int` to `long`). Narrowing numeric types is possible but can lead to runtime exceptions if observed values cannot be represented by the narrowed type. Crucially, do not change the signedness of a numeric type member; for instance, changing from `uint` to `int` or vice-versa is not permitted.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/timers-and-reminders

For development purposes, an in-memory reminder service is available, which does not require setting up external storage like Azure or SQL databases.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Serialization best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

It is permissible to widen numeric member types (e.g., `sbyte` to `short` to `int` to `long`). Narrowing numeric member types is also possible, but it can lead to a runtime exception if observed values cannot be represented by the narrowed type. Crucially, the signedness of a numeric type member must not be changed; for instance, converting between `uint` and `int` is not allowed.

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

The `main.bicep` file orchestrates the deployment by delegating to other Bicep modules. It references an existing ACR resource, defines modules for the container app environment (`environment.bicep`), storage (`storage.bicep`), and the container app itself (`container-app.bicep`). It also declares shared environment variables for the silo module and outputs the final Azure Container App (ACA) URL.

### Orleans NuGet Packages > Development and testing

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

For development and testing, Orleans offers libraries for hosting silos in testing projects and test kits for transactions and serialization.

--------------------------------

### Azure Queue streams overview > Tuning

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/streams-implementation/azure-queue-streams

When tuning the configuration for production, consider that most settings are per queue. Increasing the number of queues can distribute the load for a high volume of streams. The primary bottleneck for message processing is the rate of events sent on a single stream. A balanced configuration for cache time and visibility time is recommended, where visibility time should be approximately double the expected message cache duration.

--------------------------------

### Application parts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/client-configuration

Application parts allow the client to discover and load assemblies that contain grains and other application code. This is configured using the ConfigureApplicationParts method on the client builder.

--------------------------------

### Serializing record types

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

To exclude primary constructor parameters from being automatically included as serializable fields in a record type, use the `[GenerateSerializer(IncludePrimaryConstructorParameters = false)]` attribute.

--------------------------------

### Scheduling overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/scheduler

Orleans features two primary forms of scheduling relevant to grains: Request scheduling, which handles incoming grain calls according to specific rules, and Task scheduling, which manages the execution of synchronous code blocks in a single-threaded manner. All grain code executes on the grain's task scheduler, ensuring that even if multiple requests are allowed to execute concurrently, they are processed sequentially, not in parallel.

--------------------------------

### Deployment overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

Orleans applications require network connectivity between silos for efficient scaling. Azure App Service supports this by enabling virtual network integration and configuring private network ports for app instances. Key steps include enabling VNet integration, configuring the private port count using Azure CLI or Bicep, and ensuring hosts listen on all IP addresses for Linux deployments.

--------------------------------

### Student projects

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

Student education projects involve ideas for applications built on Orleans or new Orleans capabilities. These are ideal for advanced undergraduate or graduate courses focused on Cloud Computing and distributed technologies, providing hands-on experience.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

Bicep files utilize `param` for input parameters and `output` for declared outputs. For instance, the storage Bicep file defines its connection string as an output, which is then used by the silo Bicep file.

--------------------------------

### Add serialization methods to a type > Serializer

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Serializer methods, marked with SerializerMethodAttribute, are responsible for writing object data to a byte stream. They take the input object, an ISerializationContext, and an optional expected type. To serialize sub-objects, use SerializationManager.SerializeInner. Data is written using the StreamWriter property of the context.

--------------------------------

### Best practices in Orleans > Grains overview

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

Grains in Orleans are distributed, virtual, and asynchronous. They are loosely coupled, isolated, and primarily independent, with each grain encapsulating its state and failing independently. Avoid chatty communication between grains as direct memory use is less expensive than message passing, and consider combining highly chatty grains into a single grain. Also, avoid bottleneck grains like single coordinators or registries.

--------------------------------

### Orleans startup tasks

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

While Orleans startup tasks are still supported, it is recommended to use `BackgroundService` or `IHostedService` as they represent the standard .NET hosting mechanism for running background tasks.

--------------------------------

### Migration Guide > Migration from Orleans 8.0 to 9.0

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

Upgrading from Orleans 8.x to 9.0 includes several key changes: a stronger consistency grain directory, full CancellationToken support in grain methods, memory-based activation shedding for automatic grain deactivation under memory pressure, a faster membership protocol with a reduced failure detection time from 10 minutes to 90 seconds, and a default placement strategy change to ResourceOptimized (9.2+).

--------------------------------

### Hints for writing serializers and deserializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

A straightforward method for creating serializers and deserializers involves serializing data into a byte array, writing the array's length followed by the array itself to the stream, and then reversing this process during deserialization. This technique is suitable for data types that can be represented compactly and lack duplicated sub-objects, thus avoiding concerns about object identity.

--------------------------------

### Deployment overview > Configure private port count using Azure CLI

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

To configure the private port count for Orleans applications in Azure App Service, use the Azure CLI command `az webapp config set`. This command allows you to specify the number of private ports allocated to each app instance, which is crucial for silo communication. The command requires resource group name, subscription ID, and the app service app name.

--------------------------------

### Heterogeneous silos overview > Limitations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/heterogeneous-silos

Heterogeneous silos have several limitations: clients are not notified of changes in supported grain types, which can lead to runtime errors if a silo hosting a required grain type leaves the cluster or if a client connects before a new silo with a specific grain type joins. Stateless grains are not supported in heterogeneous deployments, meaning all silos must host the same set of stateless grains. Additionally, only explicit subscriptions are supported for Orleans Streams, as ImplicitStreamSubscriptionAttribute is not compatible.

--------------------------------

### Example

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/client

The client interacts with player and game grains to retrieve game information and subscribe for updates. It uses a polling mechanism with a delay to wait for the player to join a game.

--------------------------------

### ADO.NET persistence rationale

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

The core principles for ADO.NET-backed persistence storage are to ensure business-critical data remains safe and accessible despite evolving data formats and code, and to leverage vendor-specific and storage-specific functionalities. This involves implementing goals that allow the shape of data in storage to evolve over time.

--------------------------------

### Deployment overview > Configure host networking

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

After configuring Azure App Service with virtual network integration and private ports, two environment variables, `WEBSITE_PRIVATE_IP` and `WEBSITE_PRIVATE_PORTS`, become available to your app processes. `WEBSITE_PRIVATE_IP` provides an IP address routable within the VNet for inter-host communication, while `WEBSITE_PRIVATE_PORTS` lists the ports on that IP address routed to the app instance. For correct binding, instruct the host to listen on all internal addresses by setting `listenOnAnyHostAddress: true` in the `ConfigureEndpoints` method.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Versioning rules > Numerics

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

When modifying numeric fields for version tolerance, you cannot change their signedness (e.g., `int` to `uint`). However, you can change the width of numeric fields, such as converting `int` to `long`. Narrowing conversions (e.g., `long` to `int`) are supported but will throw an exception if the runtime value exceeds the capacity of the narrowed type. Specific rules apply to conversions involving `ulong`, `ushort`, `double`, `float`, and `decimal` to prevent overflow or loss of precision.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The storage resource is provisioned using the `Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts@2021-08-01` type, with a `StorageV2` kind and `Standard_LRS` SKU. Its connection string is outputted for use by other resources.

### Failure handling > Strategy parameters

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/handling-failures

Failure handling strategies often involve application-defined parameters, such as the maximum number of retries per minute or the necessity of completing a critical action like user login before proceeding with other requests.

--------------------------------

### Grain identities

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

Grain identities in Orleans 7.0 are now represented as a string in the format 'type/key', simplifying their structure. Previously, GrainIds used a compound type to support various key types (Guid, long, string, Guid+string, long+string). The new system improves support for generic grain types and uses human-readable names for grain interfaces instead of hash codes. Customizations for grain type and interface names can be driven by attributes like GrainTypeAttribute, DefaultGrainTypeAttribute, and GrainInterfaceTypeAttribute.

--------------------------------

### Orleans streams implementation details > Persistent streams > `StreamQueueMapper` and `StreamQueueBalancer`

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/streams-implementation

The StreamQueueMapper and StreamQueueBalancer parameterize pulling agents. The IStreamQueueMapper identifies all queues and maps streams to them, guiding producers on where to enqueue messages. The IStreamQueueBalancer manages the distribution of queues across Orleans silos and agents to ensure balanced load, prevent bottlenecks, and support elasticity. It automatically rebalances queues when new silos are added to the cluster, and Orleans offers several built-in balancers for different scenarios and environments.

--------------------------------

### Server configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Key aspects of silo configuration include the clustering provider, optional Orleans clustering information, and optional endpoints for silo-to-silo and client-to-silo communications.

--------------------------------

### Migration Guide > Migrate from Orleans 3.x to 7.0

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

Orleans 7.0 brings improvements to hosting, custom serialization, immutability, and grain abstractions. However, migrating applications using reminders, streams, or grain persistence to Orleans 7.0 is not straightforward due to changes in how Orleans identifies grains and streams. Rolling upgrades to Orleans 7.0 are not possible; a strategy involving deploying a new cluster and decommissioning the old one is required because Orleans 7.0 changes the wire protocol incompatibly.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

Enable distributed tracing by configuring OpenTelemetry with Orleans source names. This allows for effective monitoring and debugging of grain calls throughout the cluster.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The deployment process involves packaging the application into a zip file, authenticating with Azure, deploying infrastructure using Bicep, and finally deploying the application code to Azure App Service.

### Tic Tac Toe demonstrates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The Tic Tac Toe sample demonstrates how to build an online game using Orleans, a basic game lobby system, and how to access Orleans grains from an ASP.NET Core MVC application.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Versioning rules > Compound types (class & struct)

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Version tolerance in Orleans requires following specific rules when modifying types, similar to systems like Google Protocol Buffers. These rules apply to compound types like classes and structs. Inheritance is supported, but the hierarchy itself cannot be altered. Field types generally cannot be changed, except for some numeric types. Fields can be added or removed, but their IDs must remain constant and unique within each type level, though IDs can be reused between base and subclasses.

--------------------------------

### Put it all together

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The `FileGrainStorage` implements two interfaces: `IGrainStorage` and `ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle>`. Therefore, two named services must be registered, one for each interface. This enables adding the file storage using the extension on `ISiloHostBuilder`.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Activation Collection > Enable memory-based activation shedding

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/activation-collection

Memory-based activation shedding automatically deactivates idle grain activations when the silo experiences memory pressure. This process is configured using `GrainCollectionOptions`, with key properties including `EnableActivationSheddingOnMemoryPressure`, `MemoryUsageLimitPercentage` (trigger threshold), `MemoryUsageTargetPercentage` (target after collection), and `MemoryUsagePollingPeriod` (frequency of checks). When enabled, the silo polls memory usage and begins deactivating the least recently used activations once the limit is exceeded, continuing until the target percentage is reached. Standard grain lifecycle methods like `OnDeactivateAsync` are called during this process.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The `az deployment group create` command evaluates the `main.bicep` file, which contains the Azure resources to deploy. This step is responsible for provisioning all necessary resources for the deployment.

--------------------------------

### Configuration Guide > Serialization > Versioning rules > Compound types (class & struct)

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans supports version tolerance for types, provided specific rules are followed when modifying them. These rules are similar to those used in systems like Google Protocol Buffers. For compound types like classes and structs, inheritance is supported, but the inheritance hierarchy itself cannot be modified. Field types generally cannot be changed, except for certain numeric types. Fields can be added or removed freely, but field IDs must remain constant and unique within each level of a type hierarchy, though they can be reused between base and subclasses.

--------------------------------

### Orleans startup tasks > Register a delegate

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

Startup tasks can be registered by providing a delegate using the `AddStartupTask` extension method on `ISiloBuilder`, allowing for custom initialization logic.

--------------------------------

### Orleans Core Library

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Core` package contains the core implementation of Orleans public types used by application code and clients. It's referenced by most other Orleans packages and is suitable for building libraries and client applications that utilize Orleans types without direct hosting concerns.

--------------------------------

### List of options classes

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/list-of-options-classes

All options classes for configuring Orleans are located in the `Orleans.Configuration` namespace. Helper methods for these options can often be found in the `Orleans.Hosting` namespace.

--------------------------------

### Silo Metadata

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/silo-metadata

Silo metadata can be retrieved using the `GetSiloMetadata` method, which returns metadata for a specified silo. This metadata, often stored as key-value pairs, can be used to influence application logic based on specific attributes like 'role'.

--------------------------------

### Serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

Orleans 7.0 introduces a new version-tolerant serializer to address limitations of the previous serializer, which could not tolerate adding properties to existing types. The new serializer maintains high-fidelity representation of .NET types, supports features like generics, polymorphism, and reference tracking, and is significantly more efficient, leading to up to 170% higher end-to-end throughput.

--------------------------------

### Hints for writing serializers and deserializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

If a custom serializer implementation primarily consists of a series of calls to `SerializeInner` for each field within a class, a custom serializer for that specific class is generally not necessary.

--------------------------------

### Student projects

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

Two types of student projects are suggested for Orleans: exploratory, open-ended research projects aimed at enabling new capabilities, and educational projects focusing on building interesting applications or new Orleans capabilities for courses on Cloud Computing and distributed technologies.

--------------------------------

### Add serialization methods to a type

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Serializer routines should be implemented as static members of the class or struct they operate on. Registration is based on attributes, not method names, and methods need not be public. If not all three serialization routines (serializer, copier, deserializer) are implemented, the type must be marked with SerializableAttribute to ensure missing methods are generated.

--------------------------------

### Research projects > Queries over grain/actor classes, secondary indices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

This project involves building a distributed, scalable, and reliable grain index with a formally defined query model. The index can be implemented using Orleans grains or stored in a database.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > Raise events

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

Events are raised by calling the `RaiseEvent` function. For example, to record a user post, you would raise a `PostedEvent`. To ensure an event is persisted, follow `RaiseEvent` with an `await ConfirmEvents()`. Events are also confirmed automatically in the background even without explicit confirmation.

--------------------------------

### Summary

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

As source code is updated and changes are pushed to the main branch of the repository, the deploy.yml workflow runs. It provisions the Azure resources defined in the Bicep files and deploys the application. Revisions are automatically registered in the Azure Container Registry.

--------------------------------

### Concluded research projects > Distributed log analysis, correlation, and debugging

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

Distributed log analysis, correlation, and debugging is a challenging task in large-scale distributed systems. Projects in this area focus on analyzing prior art, proposing solutions, and implementing prototype tools for collecting, correlating, and analyzing application error log data across a multi-machine distributed environment. This includes exploring efficient logging, machine learning for pattern detection, and real-time debugging assistance.

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

The Orleans serializer can handle types with `internal`, `private`, and `readonly` members, demonstrating flexibility in serializing various member accessibility and mutability levels.

--------------------------------

### Prepare for Azure deployment

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

To successfully run the deployment workflow, ensure that the service principal used has the necessary provider namespaces registered. These include `Microsoft.App`, `Microsoft.ContainerRegistry`, `Microsoft.Insights`, `Microsoft.OperationalInsights`, and `Microsoft.Storage`.

--------------------------------

### Test the completed app

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

The `UrlShortenerGrain` implements the `IUrlShortenerGrain` interface. It utilizes `IPersistentState` to store the URL details, persisting them using the 'urls' storage provider. The grain stores the full URL and its corresponding shortened route segment.

### Orleans NuGet Packages > Serializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

Orleans features a fast, flexible, and version-tolerant serializer for .NET, with various integrations available for System.Text.Json, F#, Newtonsoft.Json, MessagePack, and Protocol Buffers.

--------------------------------

### Orleans global using directives

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

When an Orleans project enables implicit usings, it implicitly uses the Orleans and Orleans.Hosting namespaces, eliminating the need for explicit using directives in application code.

--------------------------------

### Grain persistence tips > Grain size

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

For optimal throughput, use multiple smaller grains instead of a few large ones. The choice of grain size and type should align with your application's domain model.

--------------------------------

### Add serialization methods to a type > Copier > Maintain object identity

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

When manually copying sub-objects (not using DeepCopyInner), use context.CheckObjectWhileCopying to check for existing references and context.RecordObject to record the new copy. This is crucial for maintaining object identity and should only be done for class instances, not structs or primitives like string, Uri, or enums.

### Prepare for Azure deployment

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The GitHub workflow automates the deployment by checking out the code, setting up the .NET environment, publishing the app, logging into Azure, creating resource group deployments with Bicep, and deploying the application zip file to both production and staging slots.

--------------------------------

### Orleans code generation > Enable code generation > Build-time code generation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/code-generation

You can emit additional diagnostics at build time by setting the `OrleansCodeGenLogLevel` property in the project's .csproj file, for example: `<OrleansCodeGenLogLevel>Trace</OrleansCodeGenLogLevel>`.

--------------------------------

### Application parts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Supplementing added assemblies can be done using `IApplicationPartManagerWithAssemblies` extension methods. `WithReferences` adds all referenced assemblies from the added parts, immediately loading any transitively referenced assemblies and ignoring assembly loading errors. `WithCodeGeneration` generates support code for the added parts, which requires installing the `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansCodeGenerator` package and is known as runtime code generation.

--------------------------------

### Relational Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

When designing relational storage, avoid making assumptions about specific tools, libraries, or deployment processes. Organizations often have preferred database tools and deployment methods, which may require specific permissions or adherence to industry standards like script-based deployments. Understanding the target database layout and estimating application load through query analysis are important considerations.

--------------------------------

### Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/timers-and-reminders

For Azure Cosmos DB reminders, install the `Microsoft.Orleans.Reminders.Cosmos` NuGet package. Configuration involves specifying database and container names, and optionally enabling resource creation if they don't exist.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The Bicep file defines Azure Log Analytics and Application Insights resources. The `appInsights` resource is of type `web`, and the `logs` resource is of type `PerGB2018`. Both resources are provisioned in the resource group's location, with `appInsights` linking to `logs` via the `WorkspaceResourceId` property. This file also defines outputs used by the App Service module.

--------------------------------

### Properties of the membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The membership protocol offers tunable accuracy versus completeness, allowing administrators to balance the risk of falsely declaring a live silo dead (accuracy) with the need to quickly identify dead silos (completeness). This is achieved through configurable votes to declare dead and missed probes.

--------------------------------

### Orleans observability > Logging

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

Orleans integrates with Microsoft.Extensions.Logging for all silo and client logs. Any logging provider compatible with `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging` can be utilized. Application code uses dependency injection to obtain an `ILogger<TCategoryName>` instance for logging messages.

--------------------------------

### Background services and startup tasks > Using `BackgroundService` (Recommended)

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

The recommended approach for background operations and startup tasks in Orleans is to use .NET's `BackgroundService` or `IHostedService`. These services start automatically when the application starts and gracefully shut down when the application stops. Registration order is significant, as services are added to the host builder and start one by one in the order they are registered. Registering the background service after calling `UseOrleans` ensures it starts once Orleans has started.

--------------------------------

### Server configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Connection strings contain secrets and should be avoided in production. Microsoft Entra ID authentication should be used whenever possible.

--------------------------------

### Request context

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/request-context

The backing storage for RequestContext is async-local. When a request is sent, the contents of the caller's RequestContext are included with the Orleans message. Upon receiving the request, this metadata becomes accessible from the local RequestContext within the grain code. If the grain code does not alter the RequestContext, subsequent grains it requests will inherit the same metadata.

--------------------------------

### Dashboard > Authorization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/dashboard

The dashboard endpoints support ASP.NET Core authorization. You can protect access by using the `RequireAuthorization()` extension method or by applying specific authorization policies, such as requiring certain roles.

--------------------------------

### Improperly modified configuration files

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/troubleshooting-deployments

Improperly modified configuration files, specifically the _ServiceDefinition.csdef_ file, can lead to deployment failures. Ensure proper endpoints are configured to avoid errors related to obtaining endpoint information.

--------------------------------

### Event sourcing configuration > Grain class attributes

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/event-sourcing-configuration

Each journaled grain class requires a `LogConsistencyProviderAttribute` to specify the log-consistency provider. Some providers also need a `StorageProviderAttribute` to manage grain state storage separately from event storage.

--------------------------------

### Failure handling

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/handling-failures

Handling failures is a critical aspect of programming distributed systems. While the actor model in Orleans simplifies failure management, developers must proactively consider and address potential failure scenarios.

--------------------------------

### Staging environments

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

Staging environments are short-lived, test-centric, immutable throwaway environments that are helpful for testing deployments before promoting them to production.

--------------------------------

### Add serialization methods to a type > Copier

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Copier methods, flagged with CopierMethodAttribute, are used to create semantically-equivalent copies of objects. They take an object and must return a copy. For copying sub-objects, use SerializationManager.DeepCopyInner to maintain object identity context. Avoid using SerializationManager.DeepCopy directly.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/tutorial-1

A grain class, like `HelloGrain`, implements the grain interface. It typically inherits from `Grain`, uses dependency injection for logging, and implements the methods defined in the interface.

The GitHub Actions workflow is triggered by a push to the `main` branch. ### Deploy and scale an Orleans app on Azure > Get and deploy the sample application

The sample application is available as an Azure Developer CLI template. The process involves pulling the template, deploying it to Azure, configuring persistence, deploying necessary resources, and finally deploying the application. This includes authenticating with `azd auth login`, initializing the template with `azd init --template orleans-url-shortener`, and deploying resources with `azd up`.

### Application parts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

To ensure type discovery, provided Application Parts must include specific attributes. The recommended approach is to add the build-time code generation package (`Microsoft.Orleans.CodeGenerator.MSBuild` or `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansCodeGenerator.Build`) to projects containing Grains, Grain Interfaces, or Serializers. Build-time code generation is supported for C#, while F#, Visual Basic, and other .NET languages can use runtime code generation via the `WithCodeGeneration` method.

### Disambiguating grain type resolution > Disambiguating grain types using unique marker interfaces

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-references

The combination of grain type and grain key uniquely identifies a grain. Therefore, creating two grain references with the same key but different grain types results in references to distinct grains.

For MySQL/MariaDB, the ADO.NET invariant is `MySql.Data.MySqlClient`. The corresponding NuGet package is `MySql.Data`.

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Core.Abstractions` package defines public Orleans types needed for application code, including grain interfaces and classes. It must be referenced directly or indirectly by any Orleans project.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

When configuring an ADO.NET storage provider, you need to set the database-vendor-specific connection string and an Invariant identifying the vendor. You can also choose the format for saving data: binary (default), JSON, or XML. While binary is the most compact option, it's opaque. JSON is the recommended option for readability and usability.

The `logs-and-insights.bicep` file defines Azure Log Analytics and Application Insights resources. The Log Analytics workspace is configured with a retention of 30 days and uses the `PerGB2018` SKU.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

When registering a custom serializer, you can also expose an overload that accepts custom serialization options. These options can be configured during registration and dependency injected into your custom serializer implementation.

### Create the endpoints

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

The application includes two endpoints: `/shorten` for creating and storing shortened URLs, and `/go/{shortenedRouteSegment:required}` for redirecting users to the original URL. The `/shorten` endpoint takes the original URL as a query parameter and returns the shortened URL. The `/go` endpoint uses a segment from the shortened URL to retrieve and redirect to the original URL.

--------------------------------

### Reliable production deployment using Azure

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

Using a `TokenCredential` with a service URI is the recommended approach for authentication, as it avoids storing secrets in configuration and leverages Microsoft Entra ID for secure authentication. `DefaultAzureCredential` provides a credential chain that works seamlessly across local development and production environments, using Azure CLI or Visual Studio credentials locally and managed identity in production on Azure.

--------------------------------

### Custom Grain Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The `Participate` method is used to subscribe to the silo lifecycle. It ensures that the root directory for storing grain states is created when the silo starts.
