Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Server` meta-package is used for building and starting a silo, including essential packages like `Microsoft.Orleans.Core.Abstractions`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Core`, `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansRuntime`, and `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansProviders`.

--------------------------------

### Troubleshoot Azure Cloud Service deployments > How to check logs

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/troubleshooting-azure-cloud-services-deployments

Logs can be accessed using Cloud Explorer in Visual Studio, specifically the `WADLogsTable`. To capture informational logs, modify the configuration to set the logging severity level to 'Info' either programmatically (e.g., `ClusterConfiguration.Defaults.DefaultTraceLevel = Severity.Info`) or declaratively (e.g., `<Tracing DefaultTraceLevel="Info" />`).

### Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/timers-and-reminders

Reminders are persistent and rely on storage to function. You must configure a reminder provider using `Use{X}ReminderService` extension methods, where X is the provider's name, such as `UseAzureTableReminderService`.

--------------------------------

### Read state

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

When reading a grain state, the filename is constructed using the grain type and reference, combined with the root directory. If the file does not exist, a new instance of the state is created. The ETag is set using the file's last write time.

To test the application, start it and then use the '/shorten' endpoint in your browser by providing a URL query parameter (e.g., `{localhost}/shorten?url=https://learn.microsoft.com`). This will generate and return a shortened URL. Pasting this shortened URL into the browser will redirect you to the original URL.

Orleans is available through a collection of NuGet packages, each providing access to different features. For this quickstart, the Microsoft.Orleans.Server NuGet package is added to the application.

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

The `environment.bicep` file provisions Azure Log Analytics and Application Insights resources. It defines an `appInsights` resource of type `web` and a `logs` resource of type `PerGB2018`, both located in the resource group's location. The `appInsights` resource is linked to the `logs` resource via the `WorkspaceResourceId` property. This file also outputs the environment ID, Application Insights instrumentation key, and connection string.

### Application parts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Application Parts are assemblies that Orleans loads to discover Grains, Grain Interfaces, and Serializers. While Orleans can scan all assemblies in the current folder by default, explicitly configuring Application Parts is encouraged. This configuration is done using `IApplicationPartManager`, accessible via the `ConfigureApplicationParts` extension method on `IClientBuilder` and `ISiloHostBuilder`.

--------------------------------

### Orleans Dashboard > Installation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/dashboard

The Orleans Dashboard is distributed in two NuGet packages: `Microsoft.Orleans.Dashboard` for the main UI and API endpoints, and `Microsoft.Orleans.Dashboard.Abstractions` for abstractions like `NoProfilingAttribute`.

--------------------------------

### Example

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/client

The client configuration involves setting the ClusterId and ServiceId, and configuring clustering using Azure Storage. It also includes building the host and obtaining the IGrainFactory service.

--------------------------------

### Silo metadata

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/silo-metadata

Silo metadata allows developers to assign custom, key-value pair metadata to silos within an Orleans cluster. This metadata is immutable and can be used to annotate silos with descriptive information or specific capabilities, such as region, compute power, or specialized responsibilities. It lays the groundwork for features like Grain placement filtering.

--------------------------------

### Prepare for Azure deployment

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

The deployment process involves packaging the application, configuring GitHub Actions for CI/CD, and using Azure Bicep for infrastructure as code. The GitHub workflow automates publishing the app, logging into Azure and Azure Container Registry (ACR), building and pushing the container image, and deploying the application to Azure Container Apps.

--------------------------------

### Put it all together

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The FileGrainStorage implementation requires a storage name, FileGrainStorageOptions, and IOptions<ClusterOptions>. It provides methods for clearing grain state, which involves deleting the corresponding file from the configured root directory.

--------------------------------

### Activation repartitioning > Configuration options

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement

Configuration options for the activation repartitioner include `MaxEdgeCount` (maximum communication links to track, affecting accuracy and memory), `MinRoundPeriod` and `MaxRoundPeriod` (time between repartitioning rounds), `RecoveryPeriod` (time a silo waits after migration), and `AnchoringFilterEnabled` (to avoid migrating well-partitioned grains).

--------------------------------

### Grain placement filtering > How placement filtering works > Ordering

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement-filtering

When multiple filters are defined for a grain type, their execution order is critical and must be explicitly configured using the `order:` parameter. This ensures consistent behavior, as the runtime might retrieve attributes in a different order than they appear in the source code. Each order value must be unique to establish a clear sequence.

--------------------------------

### Cluster Management > The membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The membership protocol begins with each silo adding its entry to a shared table using IMembershipTable. Silos use a combination of silo identity (ip:port:epoch) and service deployment ID (cluster ID) as unique keys. Epoch, the silo's startup time in ticks, ensures uniqueness within a deployment. Silos monitor each other via application probes (heartbeats) sent as direct messages over TCP sockets, ensuring probes correlate with network and server health. Each silo probes a configurable set of other silos by calculating consistent hashes on their identities to form a virtual ring.

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans supports inheritance in serialization by serializing each layer of the hierarchy separately, allowing distinct member IDs at each level. This practice is recommended, as member identifiers are scoped to the inheritance level. While members can be added or removed, inserting a new base class into an existing hierarchy requires special consideration after deployment.

--------------------------------

### Writing subscription logic > Explicit subscriptions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-programming-apis

When using explicit subscriptions, the grain can optionally specify a `StreamSequenceToken` as an argument to the `ResumeAsync` calls. This allows the explicit subscription to start consuming from a specific point in the stream's history, defined by that token.

--------------------------------

### Next steps

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

To get started with Orleans, you can create a minimal Orleans application using a tutorial. You can also collaborate with the Orleans development team on GitHub by creating and reviewing issues and pull requests.

--------------------------------

### Backward compatibility guidelines > Never change the signature of existing methods

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/backward-compatibility-guidelines

Never change the signature of existing methods in Orleans. This includes not renaming parameters, as the Orleans serializer can misinterpret these changes, leading to unexpected behavior when different versions of grains are interacting.

--------------------------------

### Backward compatibility guidelines > Avoid changing existing method logic

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/backward-compatibility-guidelines

Avoid changing the logic within the body of an existing method. If modifications are necessary, it's recommended to add a new method rather than altering the existing one to maintain backward compatibility.

--------------------------------

### Serialization best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Serialization in Orleans serves two main purposes: transmitting data between grains and clients at runtime, and persisting long-lived data. While Orleans' built-in serializers are flexible and performant for runtime transmission, they are not inherently version-tolerant for storage. For persistent data, it's recommended to use a version-tolerant serializer like Protocol Buffers, configured via the SerializationProviders property.

--------------------------------

### Client configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/client-configuration

Key aspects of client configuration include Orleans clustering information, the specific clustering provider being used, and application parts. For authentication, Microsoft Entra ID is recommended, utilizing a TokenCredential with a service URI. This approach avoids storing secrets in configuration and enhances security. DefaultAzureCredential is a convenient option that works across development and production environments, using local credentials during development and managed identities in production.

--------------------------------

### Orleans benefits > Developer productivity > Familiar object-oriented programming (OOP) paradigm

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/benefits

Orleans enhances developer productivity by providing key abstractions, guarantees, and system services. It uses a familiar object-oriented programming paradigm where grains are .NET classes implementing interfaces, and method calls are treated as messages. Grains are guaranteed to execute on a single thread at a time, eliminating the need for locks or synchronization mechanisms for shared data access. This simplifies distributed application development, even for less experienced programmers.

--------------------------------

### Example placement access code

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/request-context

RequestContext data can be accessed during grain placement or placement filtering. This is done through the `PlacementTarget.RequestContextData` property, as the static `RequestContext` is not yet populated when placement decisions are made. This allows filtering logic to use contextual information when determining where to activate a grain.

--------------------------------

### Write a serializer provider

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

To implement a custom serializer in Orleans, you need to implement the `IExternalSerializer` interface. This interface includes methods for initialization, type checking, deep copying, serialization, and deserialization. The `Initialize` method is called once when the serialization manager creates an instance of the serializer, and the `IsSupportedType` method is used by Orleans to determine if the serializer can handle a given type.

--------------------------------

### Custom Grain Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The `FileGrainStorageOptions` class defines the configuration for the file grain storage, specifically the `RootDirectory` where grain state files will be stored.

--------------------------------

### Custom Grain Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The `FileGrainStorage` class implements `IGrainStorage` and `ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle>` to provide custom grain storage functionality. It manages the storage and retrieval of grain states using files.

--------------------------------

### Event sourcing configuration > Grain class attributes > StorageProviderAttribute attributes

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/event-sourcing-configuration

Providers like `LogStorage` and `StateStorage` use a standard `StorageProviderAttribute` to communicate with storage for storing grain state, distinct from the `LogConsistencyProviderAttribute` which specifies the event sourcing mechanism.

--------------------------------

### Event sourcing configuration > Configure project references > Log-consistency providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/event-sourcing-configuration

The `Microsoft.Orleans.EventSourcing` package contains three log-consistency providers: for state storage, log storage, and custom storage. These providers are accessible to all journaled grains.

--------------------------------

### Background services and startup tasks > Using `IHostedService`

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

### Cluster Management > The membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The membership protocol ensures that all membership configurations are globally totally ordered. This ordering guarantees connectivity, as new silos must validate two-way connectivity to all active silos before joining, ensuring full cluster connectivity. It also ensures consistent directory updates, providing all silos with a monotonic view of membership, which aids in resolving duplicate grain activations.

### Properties of the membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

Each silo periodically updates an 'I Am Alive' column in its table row. This entry is used exclusively for manual troubleshooting and diagnostics, not by the membership protocol itself. It's typically updated less frequently (e.g., every 5 minutes) and helps administrators monitor cluster liveness and the last known alive status of silos.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/request-context

Application metadata does not flow back with responses. Code executed as a result of receiving a response, such as within a ContinueWith continuation or after a call to Wait or Result, continues to run within the context established by the original request.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/log-consistency-providers

The Orleans.EventSourcing.LogStorage.LogConsistencyProvider stores the complete event sequence as a single object using a standard storage provider. It supports RetrieveConfirmedEvents as all events are kept in memory. However, this provider is not suitable for production use unless event sequences are short, due to reading/writing the entire sequence on each access. Its main purpose is for illustrating event sourcing semantics and for use in samples/testing.

To configure MessagePack serialization, use the `AddMessagePackSerializer` extension method. The `isSerializable` delegate controls which types are handled by MessagePack; types not matching this predicate fall back to the default Orleans serializer. ### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

The Orleans serialization framework, Orleans.Serialization, is designed for high performance, high fidelity representation of .NET types (including generics, polymorphism, object identity, and cyclic graphs), flexibility through customization (surrogates or delegation to external libraries), and version tolerance (allowing evolution of types over time).

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans' serialization framework faithfully represents dynamic types and arbitrary polymorphism, meaning that if a method is declared to accept a base type, the receiver will get the actual derived type passed at runtime. It also maintains object identity, ensuring that if the same object is passed multiple times or indirectly referenced, it's serialized only once and all references are restored correctly on the receiver side.

--------------------------------

### Cluster management in Orleans > Membership protocol configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The membership protocol relies on an external `IMembershipTable` service. This table acts as a rendezvous point for silos and clients to find each other and stores the current membership view. Official implementations include ADO.NET, AWS DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, Apache ZooKeeper, Azure Cosmos DB, Azure Table Storage, HashiCorp Consul, Redis, and an in-memory option for development.

### Serialization customization in Orleans > Serialization providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Orleans provides two serializer implementations: Microsoft.Orleans.Serialization.SystemTextJson and Microsoft.Orleans.Serialization.NewtonsoftJson.

--------------------------------

### Serializer comparison

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans Native uses a binary format, offers excellent .NET type fidelity, supports object identity, has a small payload size, is .NET only, is version tolerant, and requires no setup. MessagePack uses a binary format, has good .NET type fidelity, does not support object identity, has the smallest payload size, is cross-platform for any MessagePack client, is version tolerant, and requires explicit attributes for setup. System.Text.Json uses a text (JSON) format, has limited .NET type fidelity, does not support object identity, has the largest payload size, is cross-platform for any JSON client, is version tolerant, and requires explicit attributes for setup.

--------------------------------

### How to check logs

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/troubleshooting-deployments

Logs can be accessed using Cloud Explorer in Visual Studio, typically within the `WADLogsTable` in Azure Storage. To view informational logs, modify the configuration to set the logging severity level to 'Info' either programmatically (e.g., `config.Defaults.DefaultTraceLevel = Severity.Info`) or declaratively (e.g., `<Tracing DefaultTraceLevel="Info" />`). The `scheduledTransferLogLevelFilter` attribute in the `diagnostics.wadcfgx` file also controls which traces are sent to Azure Storage.

--------------------------------

### Orleans streaming APIs > Producing and consuming

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-programming-apis

IAsyncStream<T> implements both IAsyncObserver<T> for producing events and IAsyncObservable<T> for consuming events. To produce events, call OnNextAsync. To consume events, subscribe using SubscribeAsync, which returns a durable StreamSubscriptionHandle<T> for managing subscriptions.

Orleans supports inheritance in serialization by serializing each layer of the hierarchy separately with distinct member IDs. This allows for independent evolution of base and derived types. Member identifiers are scoped to the inheritance level, not the entire type.

### Grain storage serializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Two key features of Orleans' serialization are dynamic types/arbitrary polymorphism and object identity preservation. Dynamic types allow for runtime type flexibility, where a receiver gets the actual data type even if the grain interface declares a more general type. Object identity ensures that if the same object is passed multiple times or referenced indirectly, it's serialized once and all references are correctly restored on the receiver side, preventing unintended cloning.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Advanced endpoint configuration allows specifying silo and gateway ports, an advertised IP address for cluster membership, and separate listening endpoints for gateway and silo sockets.

--------------------------------

### View change procedure

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/grain-directory

Before new owners can serve requests, directory registrations must be transferred. This involves the previous owner sealing the range and creating a snapshot, the new owner applying the snapshot and starting to serve requests, and finally, the previous owner being notified to delete the snapshot.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/silo-lifecycle

To integrate custom initialization logic into the silo's startup process, you can register a `StartupTask` in the service container. This task, implementing `ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle>`, ensures your application logic is executed at the configured stage during silo startup.

### Load balancing

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/load-balancing

Clients connect to all available gateways and distribute their requests evenly across them. Similarly, the reminder service is a distributed and partitioned runtime service where responsibility for serving reminders is balanced across silos using consistent hashing.

### Background services and startup tasks

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

Startup tasks in Orleans are used to perform initialization work when a silo starts, either before or after it begins accepting requests. Common use cases include initializing grain state, preloading data, setting up external service connections, performing database migrations, validating configuration, and warming up caches.

### Serialization in Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans utilizes two primary types of serialization: grain call serialization for objects passed to and from grains, and grain storage serialization for objects persisted to storage systems. This article primarily details grain call serialization through Orleans' built-in framework, with grain storage serialization discussed in a separate section.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > Reading the grain state

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

The JournaledGrain provides access to the current grain state via the `State` property and its version number via the `Version` property. The `Version` property reflects the total number of confirmed events, and the `State` is the result of applying all confirmed events to the initial state. The initial state, with version 0, is determined by the default constructor of the `GrainState` class. The `State` property is read-only; modifications should be made by raising events.

--------------------------------

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

To debug storage operations, set `TraceOverride Verbose3` in the silo configuration file and use `LogPrefix="Storage"` for general logging or specific prefixes like `"Storage.Memory"` for provider-specific logs.

### Orleans NuGet packages > Reminder providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

Orleans provides a Reminders library for server-side use, with specific providers backed by Azure Table Storage, ADO.NET, AWS DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB, and Redis.

--------------------------------

### Monitoring > Streams

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

Meters related to persistent stream queues monitor initialization and read operations, including failures, duration, and exceptions. They also track queue shutdown events and the number of messages received. Additionally, gauges provide insights into the age of the oldest and newest messages in the queue, and the overall queue cache size and length.

--------------------------------

### Relational Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

Implementations must preserve input and output names and types, as Orleans uses these parameters to read query results by name and type. Vendor-specific tuning is permitted as long as the interface contract is maintained. Constraint names should also be preserved across vendor-specific scripts for easier troubleshooting.

### ADO.NET database configuration > Main scripts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/adonet-configuration

For SQL Server, the ADO.NET invariant is `System.Data.SqlClient`. The corresponding NuGet package is `System.Data.SqlClient`.

--------------------------------

### Serialization configuration in Orleans > External serializer providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-configuration

Ensuring identical serialization configurations across all clients and silos is critical to prevent serialization errors. External serializers implementing IExternalSerializer can be specified using the SerializationProviderOptions.SerializationProviders property in both ClientConfiguration and GlobalConfiguration objects in code, or within the `<SerializationProviders />` element of the `<Messaging>` configuration in XML. The order in which providers are added is significant; an earlier provider capable of handling a type will be used over a later provider that can also handle the same type.

--------------------------------

### Serialization of immutable types in Orleans > Optimize copying

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-immutability

Deep copying of message arguments can be unnecessary in scenarios where data is not modified after being passed to a grain or returned. Since Orleans cannot automatically detect this, it offers the Immutable<T> wrapper and ImmutableAttribute to explicitly mark types, parameters, properties, or fields as immutable, thereby avoiding copying.

--------------------------------

### Service Fabric hosting example > Example project

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/service-fabric

The HostedServiceCommunicationListener class implements the ICommunicationListener interface and is responsible for creating and managing an IHost instance. It uses a Func<Task<IHost>> delegate provided during construction to create the host in its OpenAsync method and starts it. The CloseAsync and Abort methods handle the graceful shutdown or immediate termination of the host.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > Raise multiple events

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

To raise multiple events atomically, use the `RaiseEvents(IEnumerable<EventType> events)` method. This ensures the sequence of events is written to storage as a single operation, unlike multiple individual `RaiseEvent` calls which risk partial writes and increased storage accesses. Using `RaiseEvents` increments the grain's version number by more than one, corresponding to the number of events raised.

--------------------------------

### Stocks

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The Stocks sample application fetches and caches stock prices from a remote service within a grain. A BackgroundService periodically polls for updates, demonstrating the use of Orleans from within a BackgroundService, timers within a grain, and external service calls with caching.

--------------------------------

### Delayed confirmation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/immediate-vs-delayed-confirmation

The `TentativeState` property in `JournaledGrain` provides a 'best guess' of the future confirmed state by applying all unconfirmed events to the current `State`. However, this state is not guaranteed to become the final confirmed state due to potential grain failures or event races that could cancel or reorder events.

--------------------------------

### Orleans Lifecycle

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

Orleans provides a lifecycle observable that allows components to participate in the application's startup and shutdown process. Components can subscribe to various stages of the lifecycle, executing custom logic when those stages are reached. This enables graceful initialization, configuration, and termination of services and components within the Orleans application.

--------------------------------

### Custom external serializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Orleans supports three methods for customizing serialization: adding serialization methods directly to a type using attributes, implementing the IExternalSerializer interface, or creating a separate static class with serialization methods and attributes. The first method is suitable for types owned by your application, the second for integrating external libraries, and the third for types not owned by your application.

--------------------------------

### Test the completed app

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

The application uses Orleans to manage URL shortening. It features an endpoint to shorten URLs and another to redirect to the original URL using a shortened identifier. Grains are used to store the mapping between the shortened ID and the original URL.

--------------------------------

### Log Consistency Providers > Choosing a Provider

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/log-consistency-providers

When choosing a log consistency provider, consider the trade-offs between performance and consistency guarantees. For most applications, the `ReliableLogConsistencyProvider` is recommended to ensure strict event ordering.

--------------------------------

### Notifications > Track tentative state

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/notifications

To receive notifications about changes to the tentative state in JournaledGrain subclasses, override the OnTentativeStateChanged method. This method is called whenever the tentative state changes, specifically when the combined sequence of ConfirmedEvents and UnconfirmedEvents is modified. A callback to OnTentativeStateChanged() consistently occurs during the RaiseEvent operation.

--------------------------------

### Relational Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

Most queries are single-row inserts or updates. UPDATE queries can be replaced with INSERT if associated SELECT queries confirm the last write.

--------------------------------

### Bank Account

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The Bank Account sample simulates bank accounts and uses ACID transactions to transfer amounts between accounts, demonstrating how to use Orleans Transactions for safe operations involving multiple stateful grains with ACID guarantees and serializable isolation.

--------------------------------

### Tutorial: Create a minimal Orleans application

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/tutorial-1

For production-ready Orleans applications, consider using .NET Aspire to simplify resource management, service discovery, observability, and deployment. Aspire handles configuration for clustering, grain storage, reminders, and streaming automatically.

--------------------------------

### Grain directory implementation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/grain-directory

The grain directory in Orleans functions as a key-value store, mapping grain identifiers to registration entries that indicate the active silo hosting the grain. While Orleans offers a default in-memory implementation, the system is designed for pluggability, allowing custom `IGrainDirectory` implementations for different storage backends or consistency models. Configuration can be done on a per-grain-type basis. Orleans optimizes performance by caching directory lookups locally within each silo, reducing the need for remote reads.

--------------------------------

### Orleans benefits > Transparent scalability by default

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/benefits

The Orleans programming model facilitates scaling applications or services by orders of magnitude. It incorporates proven best practices and patterns and provides an efficient implementation of underlying system functionalities to achieve scalability and performance.

--------------------------------

### Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/timers-and-reminders

When configuring Redis for reminders, the `RedisReminderTableOptions` class offers options like `ConfigurationOptions` for StackExchange.Redis client configuration and `EntryExpiry` for optional expiration times of reminder entries.

--------------------------------

### The membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The Orleans membership protocol uses a special membership-version row in a storage table to track changes. Every write operation, such as suspicion, death declarations, or joins, increments this version number. All writes are serialized through this row using atomic updates, ensuring a total ordering of all membership changes. When a silo updates another silo's status, it reads the latest table state and then atomically updates the target silo's row and increments the version number. If this atomic update fails due to concurrent modifications, the operation retries with exponential backoff.

--------------------------------

### Dashboard > Grain profiler options

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/dashboard

The grain profiler collects method-level performance data. It can be configured using `GrainProfilerOptions`, including options to continuously collect data even when the dashboard is inactive (`TraceAlways`) or to set a time after which profiling stops if the dashboard is inactive (`DeactivationTime`).

--------------------------------

### Server configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Using a `TokenCredential` with a service URI is the recommended approach for authentication, avoiding secrets in configuration and leveraging Microsoft Entra ID for secure authentication. `DefaultAzureCredential` provides a credential chain that works across local development and production environments, using Azure CLI or Visual Studio credentials locally and managed identity in production.

--------------------------------

### Properties of the membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

Reliable persistent storage for `IMembershipTable` is essential for two primary purposes: acting as a rendezvous point for silos and clients to discover each other, and coordinating agreement on the cluster's membership view. This storage mechanism, along with its concurrency control, helps establish consensus on which silos are alive and which are dead, effectively outsourcing the distributed consensus problem to the cloud platform.

--------------------------------

### Troubleshoot Azure Cloud Service deployments > How to check logs

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/troubleshooting-azure-cloud-services-deployments

The `scheduledTransferLogLevelFilter` attribute in the `Logs` element of the _diagnostics.wadcfgx_ file acts as an additional trace filter, determining which traces are sent to the `WADLogsTable` in Azure Storage. Ensure it is set to 'Information' if needed.

--------------------------------

### Get started

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence

Before a grain can use persistence, you must configure a storage provider on the silo. Supported configuration methods include managed identity (recommended for production) and connection strings. Using DefaultAzureCredential with a URI endpoint is the recommended approach for production environments.

--------------------------------

### Create the URL shortener grain

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

The `UrlShortenerGrain` class inherits from `Grain` and implements the `IUrlShortenerGrain` interface. It utilizes Orleans' `IPersistentState<TState>` interface to manage reading and writing state values to the configured silo storage. This allows grains to persist their state, such as URL mappings, across operations.

--------------------------------

### Transactions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/transactions

To enable transactions for a grain method, mark it with the TransactionAttribute and specify a TransactionOption. The TransactionOption determines how the method call behaves within a transactional environment. Options include Create (always starts a new transaction), Join (requires an existing transaction), CreateOrJoin (uses existing or creates new), Suppress (not transactional but can be called within a transaction), Supported (not transactional but supports transactions), and NotAllowed (not transactional and cannot be called within a transaction).

--------------------------------

### The membership protocol > IAmAlive writes for diagnostics and disaster recovery

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

Each silo periodically updates an 'I Am Alive' timestamp in its table row for diagnostics and disaster recovery. This timestamp, updated by default every 30 seconds, allows administrators to check cluster liveness. For disaster recovery, if a silo hasn't updated its timestamp for a configured number of periods (defaulting to 3 missed updates), new silos will ignore it during startup connectivity checks, aiding cluster recovery from improper silo cleanup.

--------------------------------

### Custom grain storage provider > Initialize the storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

To initialize custom grain storage, subscribe to the ServiceLifecycleStage.ApplicationServices stage with an `onStart` function. This function should conditionally create the root directory for storing grain states if it doesn't already exist. A common function should be provided to construct the filename, ensuring uniqueness per service, grain ID, and grain type.

--------------------------------

### Publish messages to a broadcast channel

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/broadcast-channel

When creating a channel identifier for broadcast channels, use ChannelId.Create with the channel name and Guid.Empty for the namespace. Guid.Empty signifies a single shared broadcast where all subscribers receive all messages.

--------------------------------

### Azure Storage grain persistence > Configure Azure Table Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/azure-storage

Using a `TokenCredential` with a service URI is the recommended approach for authentication, as it avoids storing secrets in configuration and leverages Microsoft Entra ID for secure authentication. `DefaultAzureCredential` provides a credential chain that works seamlessly across local development and production environments.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle participation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Application logic can participate in a grain's lifecycle either directly within the grain by overriding the `Participate` method or indirectly through components accessed via the grain activation context.

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

By default, Orleans serializes types by encoding their full name. To make serialization resilient to class renaming or relocation between assemblies, you can use the Orleans.AliasAttribute. Type aliases are globally scoped, and duplicate aliases are not permitted within an application. For generic types, the alias must include the number of generic parameters, preceded by a backtick. For example, `MyGenericType<T, U>` could be aliased as `[Alias("mytype`2")]`.

--------------------------------

### Custom grain storage > Get started

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

When configuring a custom storage provider, the storageName parameter in the constructor specifies which grains will utilize this provider, often set using an attribute like [StorageProvider(ProviderName = "File")]. The clusterOptions parameter is used to retrieve the ServiceId.

--------------------------------

### Serializing `record` types

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans supports serializing C# record types. Members defined in a record's primary constructor have implicit IDs by default. It's important not to change the parameter order for an already deployed type, as this breaks compatibility with previous versions and serialized instances. Members defined in the body of a record type do not share identities with primary constructor parameters.

--------------------------------

### Concluded research projects > Horton - Distributed Graph Database

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

Horton was a research project focused on building a system to store, manage, and query large-scale distributed graphs, implemented entirely as an Orleans application.

--------------------------------

### Client SDK > Implementation details

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/consul-deployment

The Membership Table Provider utilizes Consul's Key/Value store with Check-And-Set (CAS) operations. Each silo registers two key-value entries: one for silo details and another for its last alive timestamp. Writes to the table use CAS for concurrency control, essential for Orleans's Cluster Management Protocol.

--------------------------------

### Silo metadata > Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/silo-metadata

Silo metadata can be configured using either .NET configuration files (like appsettings.json) or directly in code within the Silo host builder. If both methods are used, direct code configuration overrides any conflicting values from the .NET configuration.

--------------------------------

### Metrics

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

Metrics are numerical measurements reported over time, primarily used for monitoring application health and generating alerts. Orleans utilizes the System.Diagnostics.Metrics APIs for metric collection, exposing them via OpenTelemetry to various monitoring systems. The `dotnet counters` tool can be used to monitor these metrics without code changes.

--------------------------------

### Tutorial: Create a minimal Orleans application > Add Orleans NuGet packages

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/tutorial-1

The following NuGet packages are required for the Silo, Client, Grain Interfaces, and Grains projects: `Microsoft.Orleans.Server`, `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Console`, `Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Client`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk`, and `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions`. `Microsoft.Orleans.Server`, `Microsoft.Orleans.Client`, and `Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk` are metapackages that include common dependencies.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > State transition methods

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

The exact timing of state transition method calls depends on the log-consistency provider and its configuration. Some providers replay event sequences on grain load, allowing radical changes to `GrainState` and transition methods as long as events are deserializable. Others, like Orleans.EventSourcing.StateStorage, only persist the `GrainState` object, requiring it to remain deserializable.

--------------------------------

### Event sourcing configuration > Configure project references > Grain interfaces

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/event-sourcing-configuration

Grain interfaces only depend on the `Microsoft.Orleans.Core` package as they are independent of the implementation.

--------------------------------

### Unit testing with Orleans > Use the `InProcessTestCluster` (recommended)

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/testing

The `InProcessTestCluster` is the recommended testing infrastructure for Orleans. It provides a streamlined, delegate-based API for configuring test clusters, making it easier to share services between your tests and the cluster. Key advantages include delegate-based configuration, shared service instances, less boilerplate code, and simpler dependency injection.

--------------------------------

### Research projects > Large scale simulations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

Orleans is well-suited for building large-scale simulations, and this project explores its use for various simulation types, such as protein interactions, network simulations, and simulated annealing.

--------------------------------

### Orleans NuGet Packages > Additional packages

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

Additional Orleans packages include analyzers, code generation libraries, event sourcing base types, and support for secure communication using TLS. Broadcast channels are also supported through a dedicated package.

--------------------------------

### Serialization > Best Practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

It's important to ensure that all grains and client applications use compatible serialization configurations. Incompatible configurations can lead to serialization errors and prevent communication between grains or between clients and the Orleans cluster.

--------------------------------

### Create a grain service

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grainservices

To ensure a grain service and its client are operational, they must be configured within the silo. This configuration step is necessary for the silo to properly start the GrainService.

--------------------------------

### Initialize the storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

To initialize custom grain storage, register an `Init` function on the `ApplicationServices` lifecycle. This function configures JSON serializer settings and creates the directory for storing grain states if it doesn't exist.

--------------------------------

### Service Fabric hosting example > Example project

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/service-fabric

The Program.cs file for a stateless Service Fabric project registers the OrleansHostedStatelessService with the Service Runtime. This registration maps a service type name defined in the ServiceManifest.XML file to the .NET class. The ServiceRuntime.RegisterServiceAsync method takes the service type name and a factory function that creates an instance of the service, passing it the necessary context and a delegate to create the IHost.

--------------------------------

### Replicated grains

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/replicated-instances

Multiple instances of the same grain can be active simultaneously. The `JournaledGrain` class is designed to facilitate replicated instances by using log-consistency providers. These providers ensure that all instances agree on the same sequence of events, maintaining consistent versions of grain state, resolving racing events, and notifying other instances when an event is raised.

--------------------------------

### Additional packages > Orleans Event-Sourcing

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The Microsoft.Orleans.EventSourcing NuGet package provides a set of base types for creating grain classes with event-sourced state.

--------------------------------

### Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL grain persistence > Configure clustering provider

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/azure-cosmos-db

To configure the clustering provider, use the `HostingExtensions.UseCosmosClustering` extension method. This method allows customization of database/container names and throughput, enabling resource creation, and configuring client credentials.

--------------------------------

### Cluster Management > Orleans clients

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

For Orleans clients, configure gateway discovery using `UseCosmosGatewayListProvider` and provide the Cosmos DB endpoint and credentials.

--------------------------------

### Server Configuration > Endpoints

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/server-configuration

Silo endpoints are categorized into two types: silo-to-silo for inter-silo communication within a cluster, and client-to-silo (gateway) for communication between clients and silos.

--------------------------------

### Cluster management in Orleans > Membership protocol configuration > When to adjust settings

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

Adjust membership protocol settings when dealing with high-latency networks by increasing `ProbeTimeout`, or for critical availability requirements by decreasing `DeathVoteExpirationTimeout` (with caution for false positives).

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > Retrieve the event sequence

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

The `RetrieveConfirmedEvents(int fromVersion, int toVersion)` method allows fetching a specific range of confirmed events. This method is not universally supported by all log-consistency providers and may throw a `NotSupportedException`. To retrieve all events up to the latest confirmed version, call `RetrieveConfirmedEvents(0, Version)`. Only confirmed events can be retrieved, and `toVersion` cannot exceed the current `Version`.

--------------------------------

### ADO.NET database configuration > Main scripts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/adonet-configuration

For PostgreSQL, the ADO.NET invariant is `Npgsql`. The corresponding NuGet package is `Npgsql`.

--------------------------------

### Orleans NuGet packages > Hosting

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The core runtime library of Orleans, which hosts and executes grains within a silo, is Microsoft.Orleans.Runtime. Orleans also provides hosting support for Kubernetes via the Microsoft.Orleans.Hosting.Kubernetes package.

--------------------------------

### Custom Grain Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The `WriteStateAsync` method handles writing the grain state to a file. It serializes the state, constructs a file path, and writes the data. It also includes version conflict detection using ETag to prevent concurrent modifications.

--------------------------------

### Cluster Management > Membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

In addition to the membership table, each silo participates in a peer-to-peer membership protocol. This protocol detects failed silos and establishes an agreement on the set of active silos within the cluster.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-container-apps

The `acr.bicep` file defines the Azure Container Registry (ACR) login server resource. It specifies the ACR name, location, SKU (Basic), and enables the admin user. This file outputs the ACR login server and name, which are used in subsequent deployments.

--------------------------------

### Example client code

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/request-context

The client can set trace IDs or other contextual data in the request context before calling a grain method. This data can then be accessed by the grain during its execution. For example, a trace ID can be set using `RequestContext.Set("TraceId", id);` and retrieved within the grain.

--------------------------------

### Research projects > Distributed system benchmark

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

This project aims to define and implement benchmarks for distributed systems like Orleans, analogous to TPC or Parallel Dwarfs, to characterize performance and scalability. A specific focus may be on developing benchmarks to compare the performance of different storage providers.

--------------------------------

### Serializers > Orleans Bond Serializer

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The Microsoft.Orleans.Serialization.Bond NuGet package adds support for the Bond serializer.

--------------------------------

### Broadcast channels vs. streams > When to use streams

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/broadcast-channel

Use Orleans streams when guaranteed message delivery, message persistence, and replay capabilities are required. They provide explicit control over subscription lifecycle, support backpressure and flow control, and are suitable for point-to-point messaging or integration with external queuing systems.

--------------------------------

### Benefits of Aspire-generated manifests

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/kubernetes

For production deployments, it is recommended to review and customize the generated Kubernetes manifests. Use external parameters to configure environment-specific values.

--------------------------------

### Example

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/client

To subscribe for game updates, the client creates an observer object reference and passes it to the game grain's subscription method. The observer class implements the necessary interface to receive game score updates.

--------------------------------

### Serialization configuration in Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-configuration

Serialization configuration is a key aspect of Orleans system design. While Orleans offers default serialization settings, you can customize them to meet your application's specific requirements. For inter-host data transfer, Orleans.Serialization supports integration with other serializers such as Newtonsoft.Json and System.Text.Json. Custom serializers can be added by adhering to the established patterns of these implementations. For grain storage, it is recommended to use IGrainStorageSerializer for custom serializer configuration.

--------------------------------

### Tutorial: Create a .NET Orleans application > Create the silo

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/tutorial-1

The Silo project is configured using `Host.CreateDefaultBuilder(args).UseOrleans(silo => { silo.UseLocalhostClustering().ConfigureLogging(logging => logging.AddConsole()); }).UseConsoleLifetime();`. This setup initializes the host with Orleans, specifies the localhost clustering provider for local development, configures console logging, and uses the console for application lifetime events.

--------------------------------

### Monitoring Orleans > Directory

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

The Orleans directory layer provides several meters for monitoring directory operations. These include counters for issued and successful local lookups, full directory lookups, and remote lookups sent and received. Additionally, there are meters for cache lookups, directory cache validations, and various gauges representing directory partition size, cache size, ring size, and the distribution of the ring range among local partitions.

--------------------------------

### Grain persistence tips > Writing custom providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

Storage providers in Orleans are designed to be simple to write, offering significant extensibility. The `GrainState` API contract dictates the storage API contract, covering operations like `Write`, `Clear`, and `ReadStateAsync`. Storage behavior, such as batch writing or deletion methods, is configurable and defined by the specific storage provider.

--------------------------------

### Logging and testing > Logging, Tracing, and Monitoring

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

Logging in Orleans can be injected using dependency injection, leveraging `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging` for functional and flexible logging. The `ILogger` interface is used to inject logging capabilities into grains.

--------------------------------

### Generate code for all types in another assembly

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/code-generation

To generate code for types residing in assemblies that cannot be directly included at build time (e.g., shared libraries, non-C# assemblies, or source-unavailable assemblies), use the `KnownAssemblyAttribute`. This attribute, applied at the project level, instructs the code generator to inspect a specified assembly and generate code for its types. The generated code can then be included in the client/silo during initialization. The `KnownAssemblyAttribute` also has an optional `TreatTypesAsSerializable` property to treat all types within the specified assembly as serializable.

--------------------------------

### Custom grain storage > Get started

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

A custom storage provider requires an options class, such as FileGrainStorageOptions, to configure its behavior. This options class typically includes a RootDirectory property for specifying where grain state files are persisted and an IGrainStorageSerializer for serialization.

--------------------------------

### Reliable production deployment using Azure

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

Connection strings contain secrets and should be avoided in production. Microsoft Entra ID authentication is preferred whenever possible. The `DataConnection` string for Azure Table storage is a semicolon-separated list of `Key=Value` pairs, including `DefaultEndpointsProtocol`, `AccountName`, and `AccountKey`.

--------------------------------

### Grain identity > Grain keys

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-identity

Grain keys can be constructed from `System.Guid`, `System.Int64`, `System.String`, or a combination of `System.Guid` and `System.String`, or `System.Int64` and `System.String`. These schemes are interchangeable as they are all encoded as strings. A well-known, fixed value like "default" can be used for singleton grain instances.

--------------------------------

### Cluster management in Orleans > Membership protocol configuration > Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

Membership protocol settings can be configured using `ClusterMembershipOptions`. Key settings include `NumProbedSilos` (number of silos each silo monitors), `NumVotesForDeathDeclaration` (suspicions needed to declare a silo dead), `DeathVoteExpirationTimeout` (time window for suspicions), `ProbeTimeout` (interval between probes), and `NumMissedProbesLimit` (missed probes before suspicion).

--------------------------------

### Big and small thinking > Distributed systems have the same problems regardless of size

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/orleans-thinking-big-and-small

As soon as a system moves from a single server to multiple servers, developers face similar challenges regardless of scale, including computation distribution, coordination, scalability, fault tolerance, reconfigurations, and diagnostics. Orleans simplifies building these distributed systems by providing abstractions that ease developer lives and help avoid common pitfalls. The distributed runtime handles heavy lifting like server failures, messaging, routing, and single-threaded execution.

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans' serialization maintains high fidelity by supporting dynamic types and arbitrary polymorphism, allowing runtime types to differ from declared interface types. It also preserves object identity, ensuring that multiple references to the same object remain consistent after deserialization, which is crucial for data structures like dictionaries.

--------------------------------

### ADO.NET database configuration > Main scripts

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/adonet-configuration

For Oracle, the ADO.NET invariant is `Oracle.DataAccess.Client`. The corresponding NuGet package is `ODP.net`.

--------------------------------

### Quickstart: Build your first Orleans app with ASP.NET Core > Create the app > Visual Studio

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

To create the app using Visual Studio, start by selecting 'Create a new project', then choose 'ASP.NET Core Web API'. Configure the project name as 'OrleansURLShortener', select '.NET 8.0 (Long Term Support)', and uncheck 'Use controllers' before creating the project.

--------------------------------

### Monitoring > Transactions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

The Orleans transaction layer provides several meters for monitoring transaction activity. These include counters for tracking the number of transactions started, successful transactions, failed transactions, and throttled transactions.

--------------------------------

### Best practices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

Always use `.WaitFor()` to guarantee that backing resources such as Redis and databases are fully operational before starting Orleans silos. This prevents potential startup issues and ensures a stable environment.

--------------------------------

### Reminder providers > Orleans Reminders ADO.NET Providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Reminders.AdoNet` package provides the plugin for using ADO.NET to store reminders in supported databases.

--------------------------------

### Typical configurations > Recommended: .NET Aspire configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

.NET Aspire is the recommended approach for configuring Orleans applications. Aspire provides declarative resource management, automatic service discovery, built-in observability, and simplified deployment—eliminating most manual configuration.

--------------------------------

### Activation repartitioning > Performance considerations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement

Performance considerations for activation repartitioning include convergence time (may require increasing `MaxEdgeCount`), memory usage (higher `MaxEdgeCount` uses more memory, but probabilistic structures help), cluster stability (avoid very short `RecoveryPeriod` values), and workload patterns (works best with stable communication patterns).

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

To enable version tolerance, Orleans requires explicit serialization configuration. Mark serializable types with `Orleans.GenerateSerializerAttribute` and use `Orleans.IdAttribute` on serializable members to assign unique identifiers. This approach supports adding/removing members and subclassing independently.

--------------------------------

### Client error code monitoring

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring/client-error-code-monitoring

Bulk log message compaction: If multiple logs with the same code occur within a short interval (e.g., >5 within 1 minute), subsequent messages are suppressed and logged as a "bulk" entry with a code increased by 500000. For example, multiple 100157 entries become one 600157 entry.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The Virtual Network (`vnet`) resource is crucial as it enables Azure App Service to communicate with the Orleans cluster.

--------------------------------

### Publish messages to a broadcast channel

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/broadcast-channel

The publishing of messages to a broadcast channel is decoupled from consumer grains. Producers publish to the channel without knowledge of specific consumers, and all implicitly subscribed grains automatically receive the messages.

--------------------------------

### Common core options for client and silo builders

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/list-of-options-classes

Common core options applicable to both client and silo builders include `ClusterOptions` for setting `ClusterId` and `ServiceId`, `NetworkingOptions` for socket and connection timeouts, `SerializationProviderOptions` for specifying serialization providers, and `TypeManagementOptions` for configuring the Type Map refresh period.

--------------------------------

### Version issues

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/troubleshooting-deployments

Ensure that the same version of Orleans is used across all projects in a solution. Using different versions can result in worker role recycling. Check deployment logs for silo startup error messages.

--------------------------------

### Reliable production deployment using Azure

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

For reliable production deployments using Azure, the Azure Table option is recommended for cluster membership. This configuration is suitable for deployments to on-premises servers, containers, or Azure virtual machine instances.

--------------------------------

### Orleans benefits > Developer productivity > Location transparency

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/benefits

Orleans provides location transparency, meaning application code interacts with grains without needing to know their physical location. A grain reference contains only the grain's logical identity, and the Orleans runtime handles the translation to its physical location and message routing. This is crucial because a grain's physical location can change due to failures, resource management, or deactivation.

--------------------------------

### Typical configurations > Recommended: .NET Aspire configuration > Production configuration with Azure Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

You must call the appropriate `AddKeyed*` method (such as `AddKeyedRedisClient`, `AddKeyedAzureTableServiceClient`, or `AddKeyedAzureBlobServiceClient`) to register the backing resource in the dependency injection container. Orleans providers look up resources by their keyed service name; if you skip this step, Orleans won't be able to resolve the resource and will throw a dependency resolution error at runtime.

--------------------------------

### Adventure game sample project > How is it modeled?

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/adventure

In the game's model, Players and Rooms are represented as grains, which facilitates game distribution with each grain managing its own state and functionality. Items like keys are modeled as plain old objects, being immutable data structures that move between rooms and players.

--------------------------------

### Client

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/client

When co-hosting an Orleans silo within the same process that needs to connect to it, a manual client creation is unnecessary. Orleans automatically provides and manages a client instance for this scenario.

--------------------------------

### Orleans startup tasks

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

Exceptions thrown from a startup task are logged and stop the silo, implementing a fail-fast approach to detect bootstrap issues early. However, this can also lead to host unavailability if transient failures occur.

--------------------------------

### Serialization customization in Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

Orleans supports customization of serialization, which is the process of converting an object or data structure into a format that can be stored or transmitted, and reconstructed later. This allows developers to control how data is encoded and decoded when it is sent between different parts of the system. Serialization customization can be useful for optimizing performance, interoperability, and security.

--------------------------------

### Silo metadata > Usage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/silo-metadata

Developers can retrieve silo metadata through the `ISiloMetadataCache` interface, which is registered when `UseSiloMetadata` is called. This interface allows querying metadata for individual silos from a local cache that updates in the background as cluster membership changes.

--------------------------------

### Minimum version

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/version-selector-strategy

The 'Minimum version' strategy assigns the new activation to the requested version or the minimum compatible version. For example, if V2 and V3 are fully compatible grain versions, a request from a V1 client/silo will result in a V2 activation, and a request from a V3 client/silo will also result in a V2 activation.

--------------------------------

### Logging and testing > Troubleshooting

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

For development and testing, use Azure table-based membership with the Azure Storage Emulator. The `OrleansSiloInstances` table shows cluster status. Ensure unique deployment IDs (partition keys) for simplicity. Verify firewall rules for TCP ports 11111 and 30000 if a silo fails to start, and check startup error logs.

--------------------------------

### Typical configurations > Traditional configurations (without Aspire)

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

The following sections describe traditional Orleans configurations that don't use .NET Aspire. These are useful when Aspire isn't available or when you need fine-grained control over Orleans configuration.

--------------------------------

### Client SDK > Implementation details

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/consul-deployment

All keys in Consul are prefixed with 'orleans' to prevent keyspace collisions. Silo membership data is stored in a Base64 encoded string within the 'Value' field of the Consul KV entry.

--------------------------------

### Quickstart: Build your first Orleans app with ASP.NET Core > Create the app > Visual Studio Code

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

To create the app using Visual Studio Code, open the integrated terminal, navigate to the desired directory, and run 'dotnet new webapi -o OrleansURLShortener' followed by 'code -r OrleansURLShortener'. This creates a new Minimal API project and opens it in VS Code. You will be prompted to trust the authors of the files.

--------------------------------

### Liveness, readiness, and startup probes

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/kubernetes

Kubernetes can probe pods to determine service health. Orleans uses a cluster membership protocol for failure detection and recovery. Each node probes others, and nodes failing multiple probes are removed and restarted by Kubernetes. Kubernetes probes check if a process is running and not stuck, while Orleans probes handle inter-pod connectivity and application-level checks. Recommended Liveness Probes in Kubernetes perform local checks to detect total freezes.

--------------------------------

### When to write a custom serializer

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

A custom serializer might offer performance gains when significant semantic information within a data type is not captured by simple field copying. For example, sparsely populated arrays can be more efficiently serialized by treating them as index/value pairs. It's essential to profile and conduct end-to-end stress tests to confirm that the generated serializer is indeed a performance bottleneck before investing in a custom solution.

--------------------------------

### Return values from grain methods

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains

Grain methods returning a value of type `T` should be defined as returning `Task<TResult>`. For non-async methods, use `Task.FromResult(value)`. For methods returning no value (void), define them as returning `Task` and use `Task.CompletedTask` upon completion. Async methods return values directly or simply return at the end for void methods.

--------------------------------

### Membership table > HashiCorp Consul

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

HashiCorp Consul's Key/Value store is utilized for implementing the membership table.

--------------------------------

### Broadcast channels vs. streams > When to use broadcast channels

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/broadcast-channel

Use broadcast channels when you need to send the same message to all instances of a grain type, message delivery isn't critical, and you prefer implicit subscription without managing lifecycle. They are ideal for real-time updates without message history and offer simple configuration.

--------------------------------

### Grain persistence > Goals

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence

Grain persistence supports multiple named persistent data objects per grain, allows multiple configured storage providers with different configurations, and enables the community to develop and publish storage providers. Storage providers have complete control over how they store grain state data, meaning Orleans does not provide a comprehensive ORM solution but allows custom providers to support specific ORM requirements.

--------------------------------

### Transactions > Orleans Transactions support

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The Microsoft.Orleans.Transactions NuGet package provides support for cross-grain transactions (beta).

--------------------------------

### Dashboard in .NET Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/dashboard

If the `/Trace` endpoint returns a 403 Forbidden error, verify that the `DashboardOptions.HideTrace` setting is not set to `true`.

--------------------------------

### Orleans sample projects > Tic Tac Toe

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The Tic Tac Toe sample is a web-based implementation of the classic game, built using ASP.NET MVC, JavaScript, and Orleans.

--------------------------------

### Orleans sample projects > HanBaoBao

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The HanBaoBao sample is an English-Mandarin dictionary web application that highlights deploying Orleans applications to Kubernetes, implementing fan-out grain calls, and request throttling using a leaky-bucket algorithm. It also covers loading data from a database, caching results, and integrating with ASP.NET Core and a Vue.js frontend.

--------------------------------

### Unit testing with Orleans > Use the `InProcessTestCluster` (recommended) > InProcessTestClusterOptions
