Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/testing

The `InProcessTestClusterOptions` allow customization of the test cluster. Key options include `ClusterId` and `ServiceId` for identification, `InitialSilosCount` to set the number of starting silos, `InitializeClientOnDeploy` to control client auto-initialization, `ConfigureFileLogging` for debugging, `UseRealEnvironmentStatistics` to use actual system statistics, and `GatewayPerSilo` to determine if each silo hosts a gateway.

--------------------------------

### Chat Room

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The Chat Room sample is a terminal-based chat application built using Orleans Streams, demonstrating how to build a chat application and utilize Orleans Streams.

--------------------------------

### Cluster management in Orleans > Membership protocol configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The default membership protocol configuration includes monitoring each silo by 10 other silos, requiring 2 suspicions to declare a silo dead, with suspicions valid for 3 minutes. Probes are sent every 10 seconds, and 3 missed probes trigger a suspicion. This typically results in a failure detection time of approximately 15 seconds. In disaster recovery, the `IAmAlive` timestamp (updated every 30 seconds) helps new silos connect by skipping unresponsive silos during startup.

--------------------------------

### Configure Azure Blob Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/azure-storage

The Azure Blob Storage provider stores state in a blob.

--------------------------------

### Custom serializer implementation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

After implementing the necessary interfaces for a custom serializer, you register it with the Orleans runtime by extending ISerializerBuilder and creating a custom extension method, typically named AddCustomSerializer. This involves adding your custom serializer as a singleton service for the relevant interfaces.

--------------------------------

### Grain storage serializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans features an advanced and extensible serialization framework that handles data types in grain request/response messages and persistent state objects. It automatically generates serialization code for these types, including those not inherently .NET-serializable, and includes efficient built-in serializers for common types like lists, dictionaries, strings, and primitives.

--------------------------------

### Grain migration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Grain migration can be initiated by the grain itself using `MigrateOnIdle()`, or it can occur automatically through experimental features like activation repartitioners for call locality optimization and activation rebalancers for load distribution.

--------------------------------

### Orleans transactions > Setup > Transactional state storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/transactions

To utilize transactions in Orleans, a data store must be configured. Orleans employs the ITransactionalStateStorage<TState> abstraction, which is distinct from the generic grain storage (IGrainStorage), to support various data stores. This transactional-specific storage should be configured on the silo, for example, by using AddAzureTableTransactionalStateStorage. For development purposes, if dedicated transaction-specific storage is unavailable, an IGrainStorage implementation can be used as a fallback, though this approach is less efficient and may not be supported in the future.

--------------------------------

### Streaming with IAsyncEnumerable<T>

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

Cancellation is particularly useful for streaming scenarios with IAsyncEnumerable<T>. Orleans supports cancellation tokens in async enumerable grain methods, allowing you to stop enumeration early if needed.

--------------------------------

### Storage provider semantics

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence

Any storage operation failure other than an Etag constraint violation must cause the returned Task to be broken with an exception indicating the underlying storage issue. It is advisable to convert exceptions into types that can propagate back to the caller, especially if the client might not have the specific persistence library loaded.

--------------------------------

### Why streams in Orleans? > New requirements

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-why

A stream processing system targeting dynamic and fine-grained scenarios must meet four basic requirements: flexible stream processing logic, support for highly dynamic topologies, fine-grained stream granularity, and distribution across a cluster of servers.

--------------------------------

### Client configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/local-development-configuration

An example of a client connecting to a local silo involves using ClientBuilder, specifying UseLocalhostClustering, configuring ClusterOptions with a ClusterId and ServiceId, and configuring logging. After building the client, await its Connect() method.

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans serialization can handle types with internal, private, and readonly members. This is demonstrated by the ability to serialize struct types with private fields and properties, ensuring that all data is correctly preserved during the serialization process.

--------------------------------

### Client configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/local-development-configuration

For local development, configure a client to connect to a loopback silo. Add the Microsoft.Orleans.Client NuGet meta-package to your project. You can later choose to reference only the specific packages you need from this meta-package.

--------------------------------

### Serializing record types

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans supports serializing C# record types. Members defined in a record's primary constructor have implicit IDs by default. It's important to note that the parameter order for an already deployed type cannot be changed, as this would break compatibility with previous application versions and serialized instances. Members defined within the body of a record type do not share identities with the primary constructor parameters.

--------------------------------

### Use Consul as a membership provider > Set up Consul

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/consul-deployment

After starting the Consul agent, verify its operational status by accessing the services endpoint in a web browser at `http://localhost:8500/v1/catalog/services`. A successful connection will display a JSON object, typically indicating an empty list for services if no services are yet registered.

--------------------------------

### Dashboard in .NET Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/dashboard

The Orleans Dashboard is a built-in monitoring tool that was introduced in Orleans version 10.0. For earlier versions (9.0, 8.0, and 7.0), a community-maintained package named OrleansDashboard is available.

--------------------------------

### Custom grain storage > Get started

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The ILifecycleParticipant<TLifecycleObservable>.Participate method is used to subscribe to specific events within the silo's lifecycle, enabling custom logic to be executed at different stages of the silo's operation.

--------------------------------

### Properties of the membership protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The protocol is designed to scale to thousands, potentially tens of thousands, of servers, outperforming traditional Paxos-based solutions that typically do not scale beyond tens of nodes.

--------------------------------

### Dashboard > Deployment patterns > Co-hosted with silo (recommended)

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/dashboard

The recommended deployment pattern for the Orleans dashboard is to host it directly with your Orleans silo. This is the simplest approach for most scenarios.

--------------------------------

### Azure Storage grain persistence > Configure Azure Blob Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/azure-storage

The Azure Blob Storage provider stores state in a blob. Configuration can be done using Microsoft Entra ID (recommended) or a connection string.

--------------------------------

### Implementation of grains

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

Use StatelessWorkerAttribute for functional operations like decryption or decompression, and when only local grains are needed across multiple activations. This attribute performs well with staged aggregation within a local silo.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain diagnostics > Monitor connection errors

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-diagnostics

JournaledGrain subclasses can override OnConnectionIssue and OnConnectionIssueResolved methods to receive notifications about connection errors and their resolutions. The ConnectionIssue class provides details about the error, including the number of occurrences since the last successful connection. Issues are categorized, and Orleans reports each occurrence of the same issue category. When an issue resolves, OnConnectionIssueResolved is called once with the same issue object.

--------------------------------

### Orleans Lifecycle

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/orleans-lifecycle

A component can implement the `ILifecycleParticipant<ILifecycleObservable>` interface to hook into the Orleans lifecycle. The `Participate` method is where the component subscribes to specific lifecycle stages, providing delegates for actions to be performed during stage transitions. This allows for fine-grained control over when and how a component engages with the application's lifecycle.

--------------------------------

### Use Orleans serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

To enable version tolerance in Orleans serialization, all serializable types must be marked with Orleans.GenerateSerializerAttribute. Serializable members within these types must be marked with Orleans.IdAttribute to explicitly define their serialization identifiers. This approach allows for independent evolution of types and members over time.

--------------------------------

### State manipulation extension registration and usage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-extensions

Once an extension is registered, you can access and modify the target grain's state through a reference to the extension. Obtain this reference using the AsReference method on a grain instance. Subsequently, call GetState() to retrieve the current state value and SetState(T state) to update it with a new value.

--------------------------------

### Immediate confirmation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/immediate-vs-delayed-confirmation

Immediate confirmation ensures that events are confirmed as soon as they are raised, preventing the persisted state from lagging behind the in-memory state and reducing the risk of data loss if the grain fails. This is achieved by confirming all `RaiseEvent` calls before the grain method returns, ensuring tasks from `RaiseConditionalEvent` complete, and avoiding reentrant attributes to maintain single-threaded execution. This guarantees that no other grain code can run until the event is written to storage, thus preventing inconsistencies.

--------------------------------

### Adventure game sample project > Why Orleans?

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/adventure

Orleans enables the game to be described using simple C# code while allowing it to scale to a massively multiplayer game. This is beneficial for large labyrinths supporting many simultaneous players, providing confidence in scalability.

--------------------------------

### ADO.NET database configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/adonet-configuration

The ADO.NET configuration scripts for Clustering, Persistence, and Reminders are available for SQL Server, MySQL/MariaDB, PostgreSQL, and Oracle. Each database type has a specific SQL script and ADO.NET invariant associated with it.

--------------------------------

### Grain placement filtering > Built-in filters > Silo metadata filters > RequiredMatchSiloMetadata

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement-filtering

`RequiredMatchSiloMetadata` filters candidate silos to include only those that match all specified metadata keys with the calling silo. If no compatible silos meet all criteria, placement fails. This filter is useful when a grain must be placed on a silo with specific metadata, such as matching the 'zone' or both 'zone' and 'tier'.

--------------------------------

### Orleans startup tasks > Register an IStartupTask implementation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/startup-tasks

Alternatively, startup tasks can be registered by implementing the `IStartupTask` interface and then using the `AddStartupTask` extension method on `ISiloBuilder` with the specific implementation type.

--------------------------------

### ISiloHostBuilder-specific options

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/list-of-options-classes

PerformanceTuningOptions: Options for performance tuning, including networking and the number of threads. ProcessExitHandlingOptions: Configure silo behavior on process exit. SchedulingOptions: Configuring scheduler behavior. SiloMessagingOptions: Configuring global messaging options that are silo related. SiloOptions: Setting the name of the Silo. StatisticsOptions: Settings related to statistics output. TelemetryOptions: Setting telemetry consumer settings.

--------------------------------

### IAsyncEnumerable return values

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains

Orleans supports returning `IAsyncEnumerable<T>` from grain methods, enabling efficient streaming of data without loading the entire result set into memory. This is beneficial for scenarios involving large collections, real-time updates, or processing results as they become available. Implementations can use `yield return` or return an `IAsyncEnumerable<T>` directly.

--------------------------------

### Custom Grain Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

The `GetKeyString` method generates a unique key string for each grain state by combining the service ID, grain ID, and grain type. This key is used to form the filename for storing the grain state.

--------------------------------

### Writing subscription logic > Implicit subscriptions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-programming-apis

For implicit subscriptions, the grain needs to subscribe to attach the processing logic. This can be done in the consumer grain by implementing the `IStreamSubscriptionObserver` and `IAsyncObserver<T>` interfaces. To subscribe, the grain creates a handle and calls `await handle.ResumeAsync(this)` in its `OnSubscribed(...)` method. Message processing is handled by implementing the `IAsyncObserver<T>.OnNextAsync(...)` method.

--------------------------------

### Orleans stream providers > .NET Aspire integration for streaming

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/stream-providers

.NET Aspire simplifies Orleans streaming configuration by managing resource provisioning and connection automatically.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > Retrieve the event sequence

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

Confirmed events are immutable, eliminating race conditions even with multiple grain instances or delayed confirmations. However, the `Version` property might increase between the call to `RetrieveConfirmedEvents` and the resumption of the `await`. It is advisable to store the `Version` in a variable before calling `RetrieveConfirmedEvents` to ensure consistency.

--------------------------------

### Compatible grains > Fully compatible > Example

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/compatible-grains

When V2 is fully compatible with V1, and the current activation is V2 while the requested version is V1, the V2 activation can process the request normally. Similarly, if the current activation is V1 and the requested version is V2, the V1 activation can also process the request normally.

--------------------------------

### IClientBuilder-specific options

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/list-of-options-classes

Options specific to `IClientBuilder` include `ClientMessagingOptions` for managing open connections and network interfaces, `StatisticsOptions` for statistics output settings, `GatewayOptions` for the refresh period of available gateways, and `StaticGatewayListProviderOptions` for defining client URIs to connect to the cluster.

--------------------------------

### Grain Services

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grainservices

Grain services are registered using the `AddGrainService` extension method. The silo retrieves `IGrainService` types from the service provider during startup. The project implementing a grain service must reference the `Microsoft.Orleans.Runtime` NuGet package.

--------------------------------

### Configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/grain-directory

The strongly-consistent directory is recommended when you need to prevent duplicate grain activations during cluster membership changes. The default eventually-consistent directory is suitable for most scenarios where occasional duplicate activations are acceptable. External storage-backed directories (Redis, Azure Table, ADO.NET) should be considered when grain registrations need to persist across full cluster restarts or when dealing with very large clusters where memory usage is a concern.

--------------------------------

### Client configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/local-development-configuration

To configure and run clients, it's recommended to use the Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting NuGet package, along with the Microsoft.Orleans.Client NuGet package. For localhost clustering on a consuming client, the UseLocalhostClustering extension method should be called. A default host builder is created, and the client is configured using the UseOrleansClient extension method.

--------------------------------

### Metrics > Orleans meters > Networking

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

Orleans categorizes its meters into domain-specific concerns like networking, messaging, and gateway. For instance, networking meters include `orleans-networking-sockets-closed` to count closed sockets and `orleans-networking-sockets-opened` to count opened sockets.

--------------------------------

### Unit testing with Orleans > Use the `InProcessTestCluster` (recommended) > Configure the test cluster

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/testing

Use `InProcessTestClusterBuilder` to configure silos, clients, and services. You can configure silos by specifying options like adding memory grain storage. Client configuration can also be customized. Shared services can be registered directly in the `ConfigureHost` method using the builder's fluent API.

--------------------------------

### MessagePack serialization

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Starting with Orleans 8.2, MessagePack can be used as an external serializer. MessagePack is a high-performance binary serialization format that produces smaller payloads than JSON while maintaining fast serialization and deserialization speeds. Consider using MessagePack when interoperability with non-.NET systems is needed, when types are already annotated with MessagePack attributes, or when smaller payload sizes are a priority.

--------------------------------

### Configure the client

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/overview-helloworld

The client is configured using IClientBuilder and various option classes, similar to the silo configuration. It uses clustering options to connect to the local silo, specifying cluster and service IDs to match.

--------------------------------

### Design goals > 4. Enable optimization of hardware resources

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

Adjustments to the storage system, including queries, should be possible without interrupting an ongoing Orleans deployment, aligning with Orleans' core principle of high availability. This allows for dynamic changes to be made based on data volume or application parameters, as exemplified by the need to heavily hint sensitive queries for large tables.

--------------------------------

### Grain placement > Resource-optimized placement options

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement

The resource-optimized placement strategy allows configuration of weights for various resource metrics such as CPU usage, memory usage, available memory, maximum available memory, and activation count. These weights determine the importance of each metric in selecting a silo for grain placement. The `LocalSiloPreferenceMargin` can be used to introduce a preference for the local silo. Note that the weight values are normalized automatically by Orleans to represent relative importance.

--------------------------------

### Configure Azure Cosmos DB clustering

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The CosmosClusteringOptions class provides configuration for Azure Cosmos DB clustering, including `DatabaseName`, `ContainerName`, `IsResourceCreationEnabled` to automatically create resources if they don't exist, and options for `DatabaseThroughput` and `ContainerThroughputProperties`. `CleanResourcesOnInitialization` is available for testing purposes.

--------------------------------

### Use Immutable<T>

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-immutability

The Immutable<T> wrapper class indicates that a value can be considered immutable, meaning its underlying value will not be modified, thus eliminating the need for copying for safe sharing. Using Immutable<T> signifies a mutual commitment between the provider and recipient that the value will not be altered.

--------------------------------

### Put it all together

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

Registering grain storage involves creating an extension on ISiloBuilder. This extension uses .NET 8+'s keyed DI API (AddKeyedSingleton) to register the grain storage as a keyed singleton. The FileGrainStorage implements both IGrainStorage and ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle>, so two keyed singleton services are registered for each interface.

--------------------------------

### Grain identity

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-identity

A grain identity is composed of two parts: the grain type name, which uniquely identifies the grain class, and the grain key, which uniquely identifies a logical instance of that grain class. Both are represented as human-readable strings and conventionally separated by a '/' character, like `shoppingcart/bob65`.

--------------------------------

### Client configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/local-development-configuration

When configuring the ClientBuilder for local development, ensure the cluster ID matches the one specified for the local silo. Use static clustering, pointing to the silo's gateway port. The ConfigureApplicationParts call is necessary to explicitly add the assembly containing grain interfaces to the application setup.

--------------------------------

### Timers and Reminders > Key differences

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/timers-and-reminders

In Orleans 8.x+, `RegisterGrainTimer` offers several improvements over the older `RegisterTimer` API from Orleans 7.x. Key differences include: callbacks do not interleave by default (unlike Orleans 7.x), the return type is `IGrainTimer` instead of `IDisposable`, the callback signature is strongly typed and receives a `CancellationToken`, and the timer is updatable via a `Change` method. Additionally, `RegisterGrainTimer` supports a `KeepAlive` option to prevent grain collection and is subject to call filters and distributed tracing.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans > What's new in Orleans 7.0

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans 7.0 brought significant improvements including simplified APIs with UseOrleans and UseOrleansClient, the replacement of code generation with source generators, and a new high-performance, version-tolerant serialization system. It also added support for IAsyncEnumerable for streaming responses, per-call timeouts, and Azure Cosmos DB providers for clustering, persistence, and reminders. Orleans 7.0 targets .NET 7.0 and later.

--------------------------------

### Clear state

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples/custom-grain-storage

Clearing the state involves deleting the file if it exists. Before deleting the file and resetting the ETag, check if the current ETag matches the last write time UTC to prevent version conflicts.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans > What's new in Orleans 9.x

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans 9.x features full CancellationToken support for grain methods, a new strong-consistency distributed in-memory directory, memory-based activation shedding, and simplified in-process testing. It also includes an improved membership protocol for faster failure detection and automatic grain rebalancing across silos. The default placement strategy changed to ResourceOptimized in version 9.2. Orleans 9.x targets .NET 8.0 and .NET 9.0.

--------------------------------

### Incoming call filters

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/interceptors

To ensure the filter chain and grain method execute, the IIncomingGrainCallFilter.Invoke method must await or return the result of IIncomingGrainCallContext.Invoke(). The result can be modified after this invocation. The ImplementationMethod property refers to the method in the implementation class, while InterfaceMethod provides the MethodInfo for the interface method. Filters are invoked for all method calls, including those to grain extensions like Streams and Cancellation Tokens.

--------------------------------

### Reminder providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

Orleans reminder providers are plugins for persisting reminders in various storage technologies. Available options include Azure Tables, ADO.NET compatible databases, and AWS DynamoDB.

--------------------------------

### ADO.NET persistence rationale

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

The ADO.NET persistence provider offers built-in capabilities beyond standard storage provider functions. These include changing storage data formats during round-tripping (e.g., JSON to binary), shaping data types arbitrarily for saving or reading from storage to manage state evolution, and streaming data directly from the database. These capabilities can be applied based on decision parameters like grain ID, grain type, or payload data.

--------------------------------

### Grain interface versioning > Limitations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/grain-versioning

Versioning is not supported for stateless workers or streaming interfaces.

--------------------------------

### External clients > Initialize a grain client

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/client

The `DefaultAzureCredential` is a convenient option for authentication, working seamlessly across local development (using Azure CLI or Visual Studio credentials) and production environments on Azure (using managed identities). For enhanced performance and debuggability in production, consider using a more specific credential like `ManagedIdentityCredential`.

--------------------------------

### Transactions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/transactions

TransactionOption.CreateOrJoin allows a method to be called either as part of an existing transaction or to start a new one if no transaction context is present. This provides flexibility for operations like retrieving a balance, which might be called during a larger transaction or on its own.

--------------------------------

### Activation repartitioning > Enable activation repartitioning

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement

Activation repartitioning can be enabled using the `AddActivationRepartitioner` extension method. Further configuration options are available through `ActivationRepartitionerOptions`, allowing adjustments to parameters like `MaxEdgeCount`, `MinRoundPeriod`, `MaxRoundPeriod`, `RecoveryPeriod`, and `AnchoringFilterEnabled`.

--------------------------------

### Service Fabric

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/service-fabric

Orleans provides a programming model that simplifies building distributed applications. When combined with Service Fabric, Orleans applications can leverage Service Fabric's orchestration, scaling, and reliability features.

--------------------------------

### Configure .NET garbage collection > .NET Framework

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/configuring-garbage-collection

For SDK-style projects compiling against the full .NET Framework, use an _app.config_ file with the following configuration: `<configuration><runtime><gcServer enabled="true"/><gcConcurrent enabled="true"/></runtime></configuration>`. This configuration is also applicable to Azure Worker Roles, though specific steps might be required as detailed in relevant blog posts.

--------------------------------

### Configure the Orleans silo project

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

You must call the appropriate `AddKeyed*` method (e.g., `AddKeyedRedisClient`) to register backing resources in the DI container. Orleans providers look up resources by their keyed service name; skipping this step will cause runtime errors.

--------------------------------

### Typical configurations > Recommended: .NET Aspire configuration > Production configuration with Redis

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/typical-configurations

This configuration uses Redis for clustering, grain storage, and reminders with multiple silo replicas. Use `WithReplicas(n)` to run multiple silo instances for high availability. Use `orleans.AsClient()` when a project only needs to call grains, not host them.

--------------------------------

### Task scheduling

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/scheduler

Task scheduling in Orleans aligns with .NET's asynchronous programming model. Asynchronous methods are converted into state machines, with execution progressing in discrete steps. Each step can be scheduled on the current TaskScheduler or SynchronizationContext. In .NET, a Task can represent either an awaitable asynchronous operation or a discrete synchronous block of work within a method.

--------------------------------

### Custom serializer implementation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization-customization

The Orleans serialization interfaces that need to be implemented for a custom serializer are IGeneralizedCodec (for supporting multiple types), IGeneralizedCopier (for copying objects of multiple types), and ITypeFilter (for controlling type loading and participation in serialization).

--------------------------------

### Sample custom placement strategy

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement

To implement a custom placement strategy, define a class that implements `IPlacementDirector` and register it using `AddPlacementDirector` during silo host configuration. This allows you to control where grains are activated based on custom logic.

--------------------------------

### Default providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/event-sourcing-configuration

You can omit the LogConsistencyProvider and/or StorageProviderAttribute attributes if a default is specified in the configuration. This is done by using the special name 'Default' for the respective provider.

--------------------------------

### Best practices > Keep certificates current

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/transport-layer-security

Monitor certificate expiration dates and renew them before they expire. Regularly update your .NET runtime and operating system to benefit from the latest security patches and protocol support.

--------------------------------

### Orleans NuGet Packages

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

Orleans provides various NuGet packages for different functionalities, including grain storage, grain directory, and stream providers. Storage providers like ADO.NET, Azure Table Storage, DynamoDB, Cosmos DB, and Redis are available. Grain directory providers include Azure Table Storage, ADO.NET, and Redis. Stream providers support Azure Queue Storage, Azure Event Hubs, and AWS SQS.

--------------------------------

### Use Consul as a membership provider

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/consul-deployment

Consul is a distributed, highly available, and data center-aware service discovery platform that includes simple service registration, health checking, failure detection, and key-value storage. It operates with a Consul agent on each node, acting as either a server or client, communicating via a gossip protocol. Consul is written in Go, is open source, and provides compiled downloads for multiple operating systems.

--------------------------------

### Configure ADO.NET providers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/configuring-ado-dot-net-providers

Starting with Orleans 10.0, it is recommended to use `Microsoft.Data.SqlClient` as the ADO.NET invariant instead of `System.Data.SqlClient`. Ensure you install the appropriate `*.AdoNet` NuGet package for the features you intend to use: `Microsoft.Orleans.Clustering.AdoNet` for clustering, `Microsoft.Orleans.Persistence.AdoNet` for persistence, and `Microsoft.Orleans.Reminders.AdoNet` for reminders.

--------------------------------

### Orleans observability

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

Observability is a critical aspect of distributed systems, enabling understanding of the system's state at any point in time. This can be achieved through various methods such as logging, metrics, and distributed tracing.

--------------------------------

### Orleans Transport Layer Security (TLS) > Configure TLS on silos > Basic TLS configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/transport-layer-security

For basic TLS configuration using a certificate from the Windows certificate store, you specify the certificate store name (e.g., StoreName.My), the certificate's subject name, and whether to allow invalid certificates (set to false for production). You can also configure callbacks for client authentication, such as setting the TargetHost for outbound connections.

--------------------------------

### Deploy new versions of grains > Use a staging environment

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-versioning/deploying-new-versions-of-grains

When using a staging environment, deployment steps include deploying V1 silos and clients, starting V2 silos and clients in the staging slot (joining the same cluster), redirecting traffic to V2 clients for validation, and then performing a VIP swap if successful or shutting down the staging cluster if validation fails. V1 silos can then be safely shut down as activations migrate to V2 silos.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The `_app-service.bicep` file configures Azure App Service as a .NET 8 application. It provisions `appServicePlan` and `appService` resources in the resource group's location. The App Service is configured with the `S1` SKU, capacity of `1`, uses a specified virtual network subnet, and enforces HTTPS. It also sets application settings for Application Insights instrumentation key, connection string, and storage connection string, which are utilized by the shopping cart application.

--------------------------------

### Example project

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/service-fabric

The ServiceRuntime.RegisterServiceAsync method registers the OrleansHostedStatelessService class with the Service Fabric runtime. The CreateHostAsync delegate creates the IHost instance.

--------------------------------

### Deploy and scale an Orleans app on Azure > Prerequisites

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/deploy-scale-orleans-on-azure

To get started, you need an Azure account with an active subscription, the Azure Developer CLI, .NET 8, and Docker.

--------------------------------

### Explore the Bicep templates

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

A staging slot named `${appName}stg` is configured for the App Service. This slot also uses the same App Service plan and a specified staging subnet. It is configured with HTTP/2 enabled, `vnetPrivatePortsCount` set to 2, WebSockets enabled, .NET Framework version `v8.0`, and `alwaysOn` set to true. Application settings for Application Insights and storage connection strings are also applied, with `ORLEANS_CLUSTER_ID` set to 'Staging'.

--------------------------------

### Transactions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/transactions

The TransactionOption.Create option ensures that a method call always initiates a new transaction, regardless of whether it's invoked within an existing transaction context. This is useful for operations that must be atomic and independent, like a transfer operation in an ATM.

--------------------------------

### Research projects > Programming model for client devices

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

The Programming model for client devices project extends Orleans to client devices like sensors and phones, enabling grain logic to execute on the client. It may also support tier splitting, dynamically offloading code execution between the device and the cloud.

--------------------------------

### Diagnostics and troubleshooting > Monitoring cancellation activity

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

Orleans provides metrics like `orleans-app-requests-canceled` to monitor cancellation activity. This metric helps in understanding cancellation patterns and identifying potential issues within your application.

--------------------------------

### Activation rebalancing > Requirements

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-placement

Activation rebalancing requires a minimum of 2 silos, valid memory usage statistics from each silo, and grains must not be marked as `[Immovable]` or `[Immovable(ImmovableKind.Rebalancer)]` to be included in the rebalancing process.

--------------------------------

### What can be done with Orleans? > Timers and reminders

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Reminders are a durable scheduling mechanism for grains, ensuring an action completes at a future point even if the grain isn't currently activated. Timers are the non-durable counterpart to reminders and can be used for high-frequency events not requiring reliability.

--------------------------------

### Streaming with Orleans

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming

Orleans streaming extensions, introduced in v.1.0.0, offer a set of abstractions and APIs to simplify working with streams and enable reactive application development. The extensible stream provider model ensures compatibility and portability with existing queuing technologies without requiring special code or dedicated processes.

--------------------------------

### Load balancing

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/load-balancing

The QueueBalancerBase component balances the workload of pulling events from persistence queues across all silos in the cluster.

--------------------------------

### Reminder providers > Orleans Reminders Azure Table Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.Reminders.AzureStorage` package includes the plugin for using Azure Tables to store reminders.

--------------------------------

### Quickstart: Build your first Orleans app with ASP.NET Core

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/quickstarts/build-your-first-orleans-app

The application uses Orleans grains and silos to manage state in a distributed manner, which is crucial for scalability and resiliency in distributed cloud hosting services like Azure Container Apps and platforms like Kubernetes.

--------------------------------

### The membership protocol > Scalability considerations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

Serializing all writes through the version row can impact scalability due to increased contention. While the protocol has proven effective with up to 200 silos, it may face challenges beyond a thousand silos. For very large deployments, other Orleans components like messaging and grain directory remain scalable even if membership updates become a bottleneck.

--------------------------------

### AppHost extension methods reference

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

The `Aspire.Hosting.Orleans` package provides extension methods like `builder.AddOrleans(name)` to add an Orleans resource, `.WithClusterId(id)` and `.WithServiceId(id)` to set Orleans identifiers, and `.AsClient()` for client-only references.

--------------------------------

### Reminder usage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/timers-and-reminders

If you only have the reminder's name and need the corresponding IGrainReminder instance, you can retrieve it using the Grain.GetReminder method.

--------------------------------

### Monitoring Orleans > Reminders

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

For monitoring the Orleans reminder layer, available meters include `orleans-reminders-tardiness` (histogram of reminder tardiness in seconds), `orleans-reminders-active` (observable gauge of active reminders), and `orleans-reminders-ticks-delivered` (counter for delivered reminder ticks).

--------------------------------

### Orleans outputs its runtime statistics and metrics through the ITelemetryConsumer interface.

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/monitoring

Orleans provides runtime statistics and metrics via the `ITelemetryConsumer` interface. Applications can register telemetry consumers for silos and clients to receive periodic updates. Several pre-built consumers are available as NuGet packages, including those for Azure Application Insights, Windows performance counters, and New Relic.

--------------------------------

### Develop a grain

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains

Before writing code to implement a grain class, create a new Class Library project targeting .NET Standard or .NET Core (preferred), or .NET Framework 4.6.1 or higher. You can define grain interfaces and grain classes in the same project or in separate projects. All projects need to reference the Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk NuGet package.

--------------------------------

### Get started

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence

Microsoft recommends that you use the most secure authentication flow available. If you're connecting to Azure SQL, Managed Identities for Azure resources is the recommended authentication method.

--------------------------------

### Configure the Orleans silo project

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

When using .NET Aspire, the parameterless `UseOrleans()` is usually sufficient. Use the delegate overload `UseOrleans(siloBuilder => {...})` only for additional manual configuration beyond what Aspire provides.

--------------------------------

### Streaming with IAsyncEnumerable<T> > Consuming the stream

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

When consuming async enumerables in Orleans, you can pass cancellation tokens in two ways: directly to the method if it has an EnumeratorCancellationAttribute parameter, or by using the WithCancellation extension method on the IAsyncEnumerable<T> instance.

--------------------------------

### Influence code generation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/code-generation

Adding `SerializableAttribute` to a type instructs the code generator to create a serializer for it. Alternatively, `[assembly: GenerateSerializer(Type)]` can be used at the assembly level to designate types as serializable, causing a build error if a serializer cannot be generated. `[assembly: KnownType(Type)]` also includes a specific type for code generation but does not raise an exception if the type is inaccessible.

--------------------------------

### Grain state persistence

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/best-practices

The `PersistentStateAttribute` associates a grain class with a single storage provider, specified using the `StorageProvider(ProviderName = "name")` attribute. This provider name must also be configured in the silo's configuration file.

--------------------------------

### Deploy to Azure App Service

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

Before deploying your application, create an Azure Resource Group. You can use an existing one or create a new one using the Azure portal, Azure CLI, or Azure PowerShell. Remember the name of the resource group as it will be needed later.

--------------------------------

### Research projects > Auto-generated front-ends for Orleans-based cloud services

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/student-projects

This project focuses on auto-generating front-ends for Orleans-based cloud services, extending the actor model to HTTP. It includes dynamic generation of HTTP endpoints, support for WebSockets and bi-directional streaming with performance optimizations, and ensuring fault tolerance for streaming sessions.

--------------------------------

### Streaming: Custom Data Adapter

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/tutorials-and-samples

The Custom Data Adapter sample demonstrates using Orleans Streams with a non-Orleans publisher. It shows how to consume stream messages by providing a custom `EventHubDataAdapter` implementation, enabling Orleans to interpret messages from external publishers.

--------------------------------

### Cluster Management > Implementation details

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The IMembershipTable requires atomic updates to guarantee a global total order of changes. Implementations must atomically update both the table entries (list of silos) and the version number. This can be achieved using database transactions or atomic compare-and-swap operations using ETags.

--------------------------------

### Configure Cassandra clustering

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

The CassandraClusteringOptions class offers configuration options such as `UseCassandraTtl` to enable time-to-live for membership table rows for defunct silo cleanup, and `InitializeRetryMaxDelay` to control the delay between retries during initialization, which is useful for large numbers of silos connecting simultaneously to multi-datacenter Cassandra clusters.

--------------------------------

### Writing subscription logic

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-programming-apis

The main difference between explicit and implicit subscriptions is that for implicit subscriptions, the grain always has exactly one implicit subscription per stream namespace. There's no way to create multiple subscriptions or unsubscribe, and the grain logic only needs to attach the processing logic. This also means there's never a need to resume an implicit subscription. For explicit subscriptions, you need to resume the subscription; otherwise, subscribing again results in the grain being subscribed multiple times.

--------------------------------

### JournaledGrain basics > State transition methods

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/event-sourcing/journaledgrain-basics

The Orleans runtime automatically updates the grain state when events are raised. Your application must provide the logic for how the state is updated in response to an event. This can be done by implementing `Apply` methods on the `GrainState` class or by overriding the `TransitionState` function in the grain. Transition methods should be deterministic and have no side effects other than modifying the state object.

--------------------------------

### What can be done with Orleans? > Persistence

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans provides a simple persistence model ensuring state availability before processing a request and maintaining consistency. Grains can have multiple named persistent data objects, and this state can be stored in any storage system. While a grain runs, Orleans keeps the state in memory to serve read requests without accessing storage. When the grain updates its state, calling IStorage.WriteStateAsync ensures the backing store updates for durability and consistency.

--------------------------------

### Orleans NuGet packages > Key packages

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

When building an Orleans silo (server), reference the Microsoft.Orleans.Server NuGet package. The Server package includes everything from the Client package, so silo projects can also act as clients without additional references. When building a standalone client application that connects to an Orleans cluster but doesn't host a silo, reference the Microsoft.Orleans.Client NuGet package. All Orleans projects, such as abstractions or grain class libraries, reference the Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk NuGet package. The `Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk` package is included with both the `Client` and `Server` packages.

--------------------------------

### Inside the shopping cart app

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/deploy-to-azure-app-service

The shopping cart application is built using Orleans, a framework for distributed applications. It features inventory management, product browsing, and cart management. The client is a Blazor server app, interacting with Orleans grains for services like inventory, products, and shopping carts. The solution includes projects for abstractions, grains, and the Blazor app hosting the Orleans silo.

--------------------------------

### Recovery process

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/grain-directory

When a host crashes without properly handing off its directory partitions, subsequent owners must recover by querying active silos for registrations, rebuilding directory state for affected ranges, and ensuring no duplicate grain activations.

--------------------------------

### State manipulation extension implementation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-extensions

The IGrainStateAccessor<T> interface provides methods to get and set a state value of type T. To create an extension, implement this interface in a class that accepts a getter and a setter delegate in its constructor. The getter retrieves the current state, and the setter updates it. The GetState() method returns a Task<TResult> with the current state value, and SetState(T state) updates the state with a new value.

--------------------------------

### Separate silo and client projects

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/aspire-integration

When your Orleans client runs in a separate process, such as a web frontend, use the `.AsClient()` method to configure it as a client-only reference.

--------------------------------

### Relational Storage Provider

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/relational-storage

The ADO.NET persistence provider supports data versioning and allows for custom serializers with specific application rules and streaming capabilities. However, this functionality is not directly exposed to application code.

--------------------------------

### Azure Storage grain persistence > Configure Azure Table Storage

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-persistence/azure-storage

Connection strings contain secrets and should be avoided in production. Use Microsoft Entra ID authentication whenever possible.

--------------------------------

### Orleans streaming quickstart > Required configurations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-quick-start

To configure memory-based streams on the silo, use the `AddMemoryStreams` extension method on an `ISiloBuilder`, specifying a stream provider name and `AddMemoryGrainStorage` for subscription storage. On the cluster client, use the `AddMemoryStreams` extension method on an `IClientBuilder`.

--------------------------------

### Packaging changes

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

When upgrading to Orleans 7.0, clients should reference Microsoft.Orleans.Client, silos should reference Microsoft.Orleans.Server, and other packages should reference Microsoft.Orleans.Sdk. References to Microsoft.Orleans.CodeGenerator.MSBuild and Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansCodeGenerator.Build should be removed and replaced with GenerateCodeForDeclaringAssemblyAttribute. Microsoft.Orleans.Runtime is now referenced by Microsoft.Orleans.Server, and Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansRuntime should be removed. Application Parts are removed, with their functionality now handled by the C# Source Generator included in all packages.

--------------------------------

### Clustering providers > Orleans Consul Utilities

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/resources/nuget-packages

The `Microsoft.Orleans.OrleansConsulUtils` package includes the plugin for using Consul for storing cluster membership data.

--------------------------------

### Testing > Add and remove silos during tests

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/testing

The `InProcessTestCluster` allows for dynamic silo management during tests. You can start with a specified number of silos, add new silos, stop existing ones, or restart all silos as needed to test cluster behavior under various conditions.

--------------------------------

### Grain extensions

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-extensions

Grain extensions allow for adding extra behavior and functionality to grains. This is achieved by extending a grain with an interface that derives from IGrainExtension, enabling the addition of new methods and capabilities.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans > What's new in Orleans 8.x

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans 8.x offers first-class integration with .NET Aspire, a Resource-Optimized placement strategy based on CPU and memory utilization, and experimental activation repartitioning for improved performance. It also introduces a new Grain Timer API, a high-performance MessagePack serializer, and support for Apache Cassandra as a clustering provider. Orleans 8.x targets .NET 8.0.

--------------------------------

### Orleans streaming quickstart > Required configurations

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/streams-quick-start

Orleans streaming requires the Microsoft.Orleans.Streaming NuGet package. This package provides the streaming functionality for both the client and server. For local development and testing, memory-based mechanisms for streaming and storage can be used, but they are not suitable for production environments.

--------------------------------

### Cluster Management > The membership protocol > Snapshot broadcast

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/cluster-management

To reduce the frequency of periodic table reads, silos send a snapshot of the current table state to all other silos whenever they write to the table. This immediate propagation of membership changes relies on the membership table being consistent and monotonically versioned. The periodic read remains as a fallback mechanism.

--------------------------------

### Grain storage serializers

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/configuration-guide/serialization

Orleans utilizes two primary types of serialization: grain call serialization for objects passed to and from grains, and grain storage serialization for objects persisted to and retrieved from storage systems.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle participation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Grains always participate in their lifecycle, allowing application logic to be introduced by overriding the `Participate` method. This method can subscribe to specific lifecycle stages, such as `SetupState`, to execute custom logic.

--------------------------------

### Behavioral notes and limitations > Error handling patterns

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/cancellation-tokens

When handling `OperationCanceledException`, it's crucial to check if the cancellation originated from the expected token using a `when` clause. This distinguishes between expected cancellations for your operation and unexpected ones from other sources, allowing for appropriate error handling and cleanup.

--------------------------------

### Configure TLS on clients > Basic client TLS configuration

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/transport-layer-security

Orleans clients require similar TLS configuration to securely connect to TLS-enabled silos. Basic client TLS configuration involves using the `UseTls` extension method and potentially configuring client-side TLS options via the `OnAuthenticateAsClient` callback, which can also be used to set the `TargetHost`.

--------------------------------

### Implementation Details > Pulling protocol

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/implementation/streams-implementation

Pulling agents use a periodic timer to fetch messages via `IQueueAdapterReceiver.GetQueueMessagesAsync`. These messages are stored in an `IQueueCache` and their destination streams are identified. The agent then consults the Pub-Sub system to find subscribed consumers, caching this list locally. The agent also subscribes to Pub-Sub for notifications of new consumers, ensuring strong streaming subscription semantics where consumers receive all events published after their subscription, with `StreamSequenceToken` enabling subscription to past events.

--------------------------------

### Grain lifecycle participation

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/grains/grain-lifecycle

Components can participate in the grain's lifecycle by implementing `ILifecycleParticipant<IGrainLifecycle>` and subscribing to lifecycle stages. This participation is typically registered in the service container using a factory function that provides the grain context.

--------------------------------

### Define a consumer grain

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/streaming/broadcast-channel

The `[ImplicitChannelSubscription]` attribute marks a grain for automatic subscription to broadcast channels. The `OnSubscribed` method is invoked automatically when the grain is activated. Within `OnSubscribed`, the `Attach` method is called on the provided `subscription` parameter. This `Attach` method accepts callback methods for handling received messages and potential errors.

--------------------------------

### Failure handling > Detection of failures

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/deployment/handling-failures

While obtaining a grain reference is a local and reliable operation, method calls can fail and result in exceptions. Orleans propagates these exceptions across silos, allowing them to be caught at any necessary level.

--------------------------------

### Client connectivity

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/host/client

If the initial `Connect` method call to a cluster client is successful, the client is guaranteed to be usable until disposed. Even if connection issues occur later, the client will attempt to recover indefinitely. The recovery behavior can be configured using `GatewayOptions`, such as adjusting the `GatewayListRefreshPeriod`.

--------------------------------

### Microsoft Orleans > What's new in Orleans 10.0

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/overview

Orleans 10.0 introduces the Orleans Dashboard for real-time cluster monitoring, stable Redis providers for clustering, persistence, and reminders, and extended CancellationToken support for system targets. This version targets .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0.

--------------------------------

### Replacement of SimpleMessageStreams with BroadcastChannel

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/orleans/migration-guide

`BroadcastChannel` offers the same behaviors as the removed `SimpleMessageStreams`. `MemoryStream`, on the other hand, behaves like other stream providers. Migrating to `MemoryStream` primarily involves changing the configuration.
